paper_title: ResearchStudio-Idea: An Evidence-Grounded Research-Ideation Skill Suite from ML Conference Outcomes

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Large language models have made research ideation increasingly accessible, yet effective idea development requires more than generating candidate directions. Researchers must ground a problem in current literature, identify meaningful bottlenecks, differentiate from existing solutions, and evaluate risks before committing to implementation. We present ResearchStudio-Idea as a reusable skill suite for this first mile of research ideation. The suite includes Paper-Search, a standalone multi-source literature search skill; Scoop-Check, a standalone prior-art collision checker for novelty claims; and IdeaSpark, the end-to-end skill that composes evidence grounding, pattern-guided generation, collision retrieval, audit, and idea-card rendering into one workflow. IdeaSpark is constructed from a corpus of 1,947 machine learning conference papers collected from ICLR, ICML, and NeurIPS between 2021 and 2025, including Oral papers, a separately tracked high-citation subset, and rejected submissions. Analysis of these outcomes reveals 31 recurring ideation sub-patterns, consolidated into 15 reusable ideation patterns. Each pattern is operationalized as a structured card containing research contexts, bottleneck types, differentiation strategies, supporting precedents, and common failure modes. Given a research problem and an evidence bundle, IdeaSpark evaluates evidence readiness, reconstructs the surrounding research context, identifies unresolved bottlenecks, selects relevant patterns, instantiates one candidate direction, retrieves potentially conflicting prior work, and performs outcome-informed auditing. This workflow transforms reusable ideation patterns into traceable research proposals. Blind automated-judge evaluations show that IdeaSpark consistently produces stronger research proposals than no-skill and generic-skill baselines while maintaining competitive novelty.

sections.title: 1.1 Problem and scope
sections.content: LLM-based research agents have made scientific ideation easier to scale. Recent systems can retrieve papers, propose hypotheses, coordinate specialist agents, plan experiments, write code, and draft reports [29, 40, 49, 18, 11, 25]. Search-based methods expand the candidate space. Novelty tools make prior-art checking more explicit. This progress shifts the bottleneck. The hard question is no longer whether an LLM can produce plausible proposal. It is whether early-stage research work can be organized into reusable skills that ground evidence, generate one defensible direction, and audit its distance from prior art before experiments begin. ResearchStudio-Idea addresses this first-mile problem as suite of three open skills. Paper-Search provides reusable literature grounding across arXiv, DBLP, OpenAlex, OpenReview, Semantic Scholar, and Crossref. Scoop-Check performs claim-level prior-art collision checking by decomposing proposed novelty into problem framing, core mechanism, key insight, and application domain, then comparing those axes against retrieved prior work. IdeaSpark is the end-to-end skill: it composes literature grounding, bottleneck diagnosis, pattern-guided candidate construction, collision retrieval, audit, and idea-card packaging into one research-problem-to-idea-card workflow. Paper-Search and Scoop-Check are therefore not hidden implementation details; they are standalone skills that also supply reusable search and review functions inside the larger ideation loop. The empirical center of this report is IdeaSpark because it is the point where ResearchStudio-Ideas search, generation, and review functions must work together. We study it as outcome-grounded skill induction for LLM research ideation. The object we induce is an inference-time skill, not an acceptance predictor, novelty score, or paper-writing agent. The skill consists of ideation pattern cards, workflow prompts, schemas, retrieval hooks, and validators that an LLM loads while generating structured idea. We ground it in public ML conference outcomes because those outcomes contain traces of research practice. Oral papers show cases that program committees elevated. High-citation papers show what the community reused. Rejected submissions show how similar attempts fail. The goal is to turn those traces into operational guidance, while keeping acceptance and execution outside the claim. This report asks two questions. First, can conference outcomes be mined into compact but usable map of ideation patterns? Second, can that map be packaged as model-agnostic skill suite whose workflow generates and audits one research idea? We answer the first question with 1,947-paper analysis of ICLR, ICML, and NeurIPS outcomes. We answer the second by releasing ResearchStudio-Ideas three skills and evaluating IdeaSpark-generated ideas with blind automated judges. The evaluation is scoped to the idea stage. It does not test implementation success, human peer review, or program-committee selection.

sections.title: 1.2 Motivation
sections.content: Existing systems address important pieces of research ideation, but they leave missing skill layer between outcome data and generation. End-to-end systems automate long research workflows [29, 40, 49, 61]. Multiagent and search systems explore larger proposal spaces [60, 17, 53, 38, 4]. Retrieval-augmented methods ground hypotheses in adjacent literature [23, 14, 56, 8]. Novelty tools, benchmarks, and pattern-induction methods check prior-art collision or name recurring research moves [55, 59, 7, 43, 41, 26, 13, 22, 44]. These components are complementary. What remains under-specified is how to turn them into interoperable skills, and how to turn conference outcomes into an executable ideation workflow: reusable procedural object that helps an LLM organize gap, instantiate research strategy, distinguish it from neighboring work, and audit the proposal before implementation. Research assistants are increasingly capable of retrieving literature, synthesizing evidence, generating candidate ideas, and iteratively refining proposals. The transition from retrieved evidence to actionable research opportunities, however, remains weakly structured. Researchers still need to decide which limitations constitute meaningful bottlenecks, which opportunities remain unresolved, and which directions are distinct enough from prior work to justify further investment. Current evaluations of LLM-generated ideas make this gap concrete. large blind study finds that LLMgenerated NLP ideas can be judged more novel than expert ideas while being less feasible [46]. follow-up execution study reports that the gap becomes sharper once ideas are carried out [45]. HindSight similarly finds that ideas rated as more novel by an LLM are less likely to match real future work [19]. These results do not imply that an idea-stage skill can solve execution. They do show that surface novelty, proposal fluency, and post-hoc novelty scoring are weak proxies for useful ideation. useful skill should instead expose how real papers connect gap to method. It should show how they assemble differentiation evidence, and how similar attempts fail before experiments begin. We call these recurring 4 procedural objects ideation patterns. pattern is not merely label such as decompose the problem or add supervision. It must state what kind of gap it applies to, how the method is constructed, what distinguishes it from nearby prior work, what evidence makes it credible, and where similar attempts fail. The missing middle is therefore an outcome-grounded skill layer that connects research evidence, identified bottlenecks, reusable ideation patterns, and candidate research directions. Retrieved papers expose observations, limitations, reviewer concerns, and unresolved questions. Generated proposals require strategic moves that connect those observations to plausible directions. The missing layer is the mechanism that transforms evidence into opportunities and opportunities into auditable research proposals. We need to know how these patterns recur across ML papers, how they compose inside contributions, and how they differ across accepted, high-citation, and rejected work. We also need to know whether they can be operationalized as reusable inference-time skills. ResearchStudio-Idea addresses this missing middle as suite, and IdeaSpark tests the strongest version of the claim: whether search, pattern-guided generation, collision checking, and audit can be composed into one faithful ideation workflow rather than another generic brainstorming prompt or novelty scorer. Section 2.6 gives the detailed comparison to current idea-generation methods.

sections.title: 1.3 Approach overview
sections.content: ResearchStudio-Idea packages early-stage ideation into three reusable skills. Paper-Search supplies the literature-grounding primitive. Scoop-Check supplies the claim-level prior-art review primitive. IdeaSpark induces ideation patterns from conference outcomes and packages them as the structured end-to-end ideation skill. Given research problem and an evidence bundle, IdeaSpark performs evidence assessment, bottleneck diagnosis, innovation-pattern retrieval, candidate construction, differentiation analysis, collision retrieval, and outcome-informed auditing. The output is not only candidate research direction, but also the supporting rationale, historical precedents, and diagnostic evidence needed to evaluate it. Figure 2 summarizes this data-to-skill flow from corpus construction to runtime use. Figure 2: IdeaSpark data-to-skill workflow. The upper band constructs reusable ideation assets from the 1,947-paper ICLR / ICML / NeurIPS corpus: papers are outcome-labeled, normalized into strategy signatures, mined into 31 sub-patterns, and induced into 15 operational pattern cards. The lower band shows how the skill uses those cards at inference time: evidence grounding and full-text retrieval feed staged reasoning loop for bottleneck diagnosis, pattern-guided candidate generation, and collision/audit verdicts, followed by expansion, validation, and idea-card deliverables. The empirical pipeline has four stages. First, we collect papers from ICLR, ICML, and NeurIPS using public OpenReview metadata and Semantic Scholar citation information [34, 42]. Each paper is labeled as Oral, High-Cited, Reject, or combination when labels overlap. Second, we extract strategy-level innovation fields and rewrite them into domain-agnostic descriptions. This separates the methodological operation from topic vocabulary. As result, clustering groups papers by research strategy rather than by application area. Third, we embed and cluster these strategy signatures, using workflow inspired by scientific document representations and density-based topic discovery [6, 47, 1, 12, 31, 30, 33]. Fourth, we induce higher-level ideation patterns from the fine-grained clusters and convert each pattern into an operational card. The resulting cards are not taxonomy labels. Each card records the research situation the pattern addresses, 5 the structure distilled from accepted examples, the differentiating mechanism relative to nearby prior work, the evidence expectations that make the claim inspectable, and the failure modes abstracted from rejected submissions. This contrast between success and failure offers insights beyond accepted-only inductions. In the runtime skill, the cards serve two roles. They guide structured idea generation, and they provide an audit reference for checking whether candidate preserves the intended structure, avoids known failure modes, and names plausible prior-art threats. Generation and audit share the same corpus-derived object, while the papers claim remains at the idea stage. The IdeaSpark layer follows model-agnostic, two-tier design. The runtime tier includes lean skill specification, phase prompts, schemas, retrieval hooks, and deterministic validators. The evidence tier consists of 15 ideationpattern cards, 31 sub-pattern cards, domain-by-pattern matrix, saturation records, and corpus-derived failure-mode inventory. This separation follows recent skill-authoring guidance: keep the runtime lightweight, use progressive disclosure for detailed evidence, and validate phase boundaries with deterministic checks where possible [3, 39, 54, 58]. Consequently, the skill is faithful by construction rather than by instruction. Every significant claim either traces to retrieved record or is explicitly marked as model-supplied. Retrieval gates and deterministic validators enforce this contract rather than relying on prompt compliance (13.5).

sections.title: 1.4 Main empirical findings
sections.content: This report supports five findings: First, the induced pattern space is compact but nontrivial. From 31 fine-grained ideation sub-patterns, the pipeline induces 15 higher-level ideation patterns. The number 15 is not canonical ontology of ML It is the operational granularity produced by the current extraction, abstraction, embedding, innovation. clustering, and induction pipeline. Because it comes from single Opus 4.7 induction call, an inter-prompt, inter-seed, and inter-model stability study remains future work; we expect comparable run to land at 1218 categories with substantial overlap. Second, rejected and accepted papers often share the same high-level pattern space. Reject-only re-clustering maps every rejected-paper cluster back onto the existing 15-pattern vocabulary, with no out-oftaxonomy bucket. This suggests that rejected submissions are most useful as contrastive evidence about weak instantiations, failure modes, and boundary cases, not as separate negative strategy class. Third, multi-pattern composition is the norm. separate paper-level multi-label pass shows that papers commonly combine several ideation patterns rather than executing single isolated move. In this corpus, = 2 is the modal composition size across Oral, HC, and Reject papers, with 33.6% tail at 3. This motivates the skill design choice to generate one to three pattern roles rather than selecting single pattern as recipe. Fourth, ideation patterns are broadly domain-covering but domain-conditional in effect. The largest patterns appear across many induced research domains, which supports the use of domain-agnostic pattern cards. Acceptance and impact profiles, however, vary by domain-pattern cell. The skill therefore treats domain statistics as audit context, not as deterministic generation prior. Fifth, endpoint idea quality is supported by an automated-judge study, but not by human review. An automated-judge endpoint evaluation (Section 14) indicates that IdeaSpark improves generated-idea quality over no-skill and generic-skill baselines. Acceptance-level claims still require blind human study.

sections.title: 1.5 Contributions
sections.content: This report makes three contributions. Outcome-grounded pattern map. We provide 1,947-paper corpus analysis spanning Oral, high-citation, and rejected papers from major ML conferences. The analysis induces 15 ideation patterns, 31 sub-patterns, and 28 research domains. It then measures pattern-level acceptance, citation, domain, conference, temporal, and multi-pattern composition profiles. Contrastive, failure-aware pattern cards. We turn the accepted-versus-rejected contrast into card content. Each card pairs success conditions distilled from accepted work with failure modes distilled from rejected work. Accepted-only induction cannot supply this object. The empirical warrant is the reject-only mapping of 10: rejected papers do not occupy separate strategy space, so their value is as contrastive evidence about weak instantiations and boundary cases. ResearchStudio-Idea skill suite and endpoint evaluation. We package the work as three reusable skills: Paper-Search for literature grounding, Scoop-Check for prior-art collision checking, and IdeaSpark for end-toend idea generation and audit. We also report blind automated evaluation of IdeaSpark-generated ideas along quality and novelty axes.

sections.title: 1.6 Report organization
sections.content: Section 2 positions ResearchStudio-Idea and IdeaSpark against recent work on LLM-based scientific discovery, ideation, novelty assessment, benchmarks, and pattern induction. Sections 36 describe the dataset, extraction procedure, clustering pipeline, and ideation-pattern induction. Sections 79 analyze acceptance profiles, domain structure, temporal trends, and conference-level variation. Section 10 validates the taxonomy against rejected papers. Section 11 reports embedding and abstraction ablations. Section 12 synthesizes the empirical takeaways. Section 13 specifies the IdeaSpark runtime and audit workflow within the ResearchStudio-Idea suite, and Section 14 reports the automated-judge evaluation of generated ideas. Section 15 consolidates limitations and threats to validity across the corpus, skill, and evaluation. Section 16 presents the released data, model, and skill-card artifacts.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: The 20242026 literature on LLM research-idea generation has expanded rapidly. We group it into the families most relevant to IdeaSpark and survey each below. To keep the comparison coherent, we defer direct head-tohead differentiation to 2.6.

sections.title: 2.1 End-to-end “AI scientist” systems
sections.content: End-to-end systems automate the full research lifecycle. AI Scientist [29] drafts, implements, evaluates, and writes up research ideas in single loop, although its output was often judged incremental. AI-Researcher [49] extends this direction with structured architectures and decoupled training pipelines for autonomous innovation. Agent Laboratory [40] starts from human-supplied research idea and produces research report and code, with variable human-in-the-loop control. Idea2Plan [18] bridges idea generation and research planning through ReAct-style scaffolding with arXiv tools. The Sakana AI Scientist v2 [57] adds evolutionary-search refinement, while Google AI co-scientist [11] uses Gemini-based multi-agent architecture for hypothesis and proposal generation. Auto Research [25] spans literature review, ideation, innovation pattern, experimentation, paper writing, and rebuttal in multi-agent framework. parallel critical literature documents why full-lifecycle autonomy remains fragile. Trehan et al. [51] distill six recurring failure modes from four autonomous ML-paper attempts: training-default bias, implementation drift under execution pressure, long-horizon context degradation, premature success declarations, thin domain knowledge, and weak experimental taste. Bisht et al. [5] argue that current agentic scientists are not yet built for autonomy. They emphasize problem-selection bias, missing tacit laboratory knowledge, output homogenization from preference optimization, and the absence of experimental feedback loops. This line of critique motivates IdeaSparks narrower scope: improve the idea before downstream execution begins.

sections.title: 2.2 Multi-agent and search-based ideation
sections.content: Multi-agent and search-based systems focus more directly on the ideation step. VirSci [48] and IRIS [9] model scientific teamwork through role-separated agents, such as ideators, reviewers, and retrievers. Deep Ideation [60] introduces an explore-expand-evolve workflow over scientific concept network, with critic engine trained on real reviewer feedback. Nova [17] adds an iterative planning-and-search loop that retrieves external knowledge to enrich each candidate and improve novelty and diversity. Other systems make search itself the core ideation mechanism. FlowPIE [53] casts idea generation as testtime evolution process driven by flow-guided Monte Carlo Tree Search over the literature. This design lets exploration and ideation co-adapt rather than run as separate stages. The multi-workflow benchmark [38] compares reflection-based refinement, Sakana-style evolution, Google Co-Scientist multi-agent reasoning, GPT Deep Research recursive decomposition, and Gemini multimodal long-context workflows. It finds that decompositionbased and long-context workflows reach mean novelty of 4.17/5. Alien Science [4] formalizes complementary creativity gap, cognitive availability, and learns to sample coherent but cognitively unavailable directions from clustered idea atoms.

sections.title: 2.3 Pattern induction from conference outcomes
sections.content: The closest line of work to ours induces innovation patterns from top-conference papers. Liu et al. [26] annotate Oral papers against predefined 12-category taxonomy. MoRI [13] extracts motivationinnovation pattern 7 pairs from ICLR 20242025 accepted papers. It then trains reasoning policy through supervised fine-tuning and controllable reinforcement learning on feasibility, novelty, and effectiveness. MotivGraph-SoIQ [22] builds motivational knowledge graph and pairs it with Socratic dialogue for ideation. Navigating Ideation Space [44] decomposes scientific ideas into conceptual representations for positioning new ideas. parallel paradigm grounds idea generation in knowledge graphs mined from large literature corpora. SciMuse [14] builds knowledge graph from 58 million research papers. In 100-research-group-leader evaluation, its cross-disciplinary graph paths help AI-generated ideas be perceived as novel and impactful. KGgrounded hypothesis generation [56] retrieves relevant graph sub-structures to constrain LLM hypothesis output and reports improved novelty and plausibility on biomedical benchmarks. Graphs of Research [8] turns citation structure into training signal. It extracts per-seed-paper citation-evolution directed acyclic graph from the 2-hop reference neighborhood and fine-tunes an LLM to generate ideas that plausibly extend it.

sections.title: 2.4 Novelty, evaluation, and benchmarks
sections.content: A separate family judges ideas rather than producing them. The first line focuses on novelty collision. NovBench [55] provides large-scale benchmark for novelty evaluation, with 1,684 paper-review pairs and four-dimensional framework covering relevance, correctness, coverage, and clarity. RINoBench [41] complements it with 1,381 expert-judged ideas focused on novelty judgment. It finds that LLM novelty verdicts can diverge substantially from expert gold, even when the model gives human-like rationales. We treat this as motivation for grounding novelty checks in retrieved evidence rather than model opinion. OpenNovelty [59] produces transparent evidence-based novelty reports for 500+ ICLR 2026 submissions. GraphMind [7] ties paper key elements to retrieved literature for interactive novelty assessment. Literature-Grounded Novelty Assessment / Idea Novelty Checker [43] uses RAG with two-stage retrieve-then-rerank and literature-grounded rationales. Scideator [36] combines paper facets and novelty assessment modules for human-LLM ideation. Augmenting Research Ideation with Data [27] integrates metadata and automated preliminary validation into ideation, reporting gains in feasibility and quality on social-science tasks. The second line evaluates idea quality directly. Si et al. [46] ran 100+-NLP-researcher blind study showing that LLM-generated ideas are rated more novel but less feasible than expert ideas. Their follow-up execution study [45] reports that AI ideas score significantly lower than human ideas after execution. The gap is therefore operational, not merely stylistic or novelty-related. AI Can Learn Scientific Taste [50] trains taste model on accepted/rejected pairs. HindSight [19] evaluates LLM-generated ideas via future-citation impact. Learning to Predict Future-Aligned Research Proposals [52] predicts whether candidate proposal aligns with future research directions. MIRAI [24] forecasts future paper impact from title and abstract, then uses that predictor to steer idea generation toward higher-impact directions. The third line packages these judgments as standing benchmarks. IdeaBench [15] evaluates research idea generation against referenced-work baselines. AI Idea Bench 2025 [35] curates 3,495 papers from ICLR top-2% and CVPR highlights as ground truth. ResearchBench [28] decomposes scientific discovery into inspiration retrieval, hypothesis composition, and hypothesis ranking. NewtonBench [62] evaluates scientific-law discovery on 324 physics tasks with counterfactual law shifts.

sections.title: 2.5 Surveys and skill-engineering practice
sections.content: Two recent surveys map the broader landscape. From Automation to Autonomy [61] organizes systems through Tool / Analyst / Scientist taxonomy. Towards Scientific Intelligence [37] surveys architectures, benchmarks, and ethical questions. On the engineering side, Anthropics skill-authoring practices [3] shape IdeaSparks two-tier design: progressive disclosure, lean top-level specification, deterministic routines for repetitive steps, and schema-validated phase boundaries. The embedding and clustering substrate is described where it is used, in 6 and 11.

sections.title: 2.6 How IdeaSpark differs from current idea-generation methods
sections.content: Current idea-generation methods fall into three families. End-to-end AI scientist systems automate the full research lifecycle. Search-based systems generate many candidates and filter them with learned novelty, taste, or impact signals. Patternand knowledge-graph-induction methods mine accepted papers or large literature graphs for reusable structure. IdeaSpark draws ingredients from all three families: pattern induction, an accept/reject contrast, and retrieval-grounded generation. Its distinguishing move is to bind these ingredients onto one object. That object is the non-parametric, data-induced pattern card. The same card that helps select move for 8 bottleneck is also used to audit the generated candidate. This differs from searching candidate space and ranking ideas by learned signal. It also differs from fitting parametric motivationmethod policy, where generation and checking can become separate procedures. Three properties follow from this design. First, the cards are induced from three-way Oral / HC / Reject contrast, so they carry an explicit failure signal that accepted-only induction and concept-level knowledge graphs do not. Second, the skill stops at one reviewer-facing idea card rather than running the full research lifecycle. This choice reflects the premise that idea quality is an upstream bottleneck for many downstream failures. Third, the audit checks the candidate against the corpus-derived failure modes of the pattern that produced it, together with retrieved prior art. The knowledge that generates an idea and the knowledge that checks it are therefore one object, not two disconnected models. Execution-level gaps are surfaced rather than masked by surface novelty.

sections.title: 3.1 Scope and labeling
sections.content: We collect papers from ICLR, ICML, and NeurIPS for 20212025. Collection uses the OpenReview API [34] (v1 for ICLR 20212023; v2 for ICLR 2024+, ICML 2023+, and NeurIPS 2023+) and Semantic Scholar citation metadata [42]. ICLR 2020 is excluded from this release because its review-data schema differs from later years. For each paper, we retain the title, abstract, author list, OpenReview id, decision string, available review fields, available meta-review, and Semantic Scholar citation count. The review fields include rating, confidence, soundness, contribution, summary, strengths, weaknesses, and questions. The three labels operationalize the contrast at the heart of this work. Oral marks what program committees most value. High-Cited marks what the community adopts. Reject supplies the failure signal that accepted-only induction cannot provide. Each paper receives one or more labels, and labels may overlap. In this corpus, 49 papers are simultaneously Oral and High-Cited. Oral (1,014 papers): papers whose venue decision is Oral, marking program-committee preference. High-Cited (HC) (260 papers, HCall): top-30 most-cited papers per venue-year by Semantic Scholar citation count, representing community adoption. For 2025, we use the top-10 most-cited papers because citation counts are still accumulating. The high-citation pool overlaps Oral: HCall = 260, OralHC=49, and HCdisjoint = 211. We use HCall for citation-label coverage and HCdisjoint when analyses need mutually exclusive Oral, HC, and Reject classes. Reject (722 papers): papers with an explicit Reject decision and non-trivial review set, representing concrete failure signal. Table 1 shows the venue-year breakdown. The per-venue counts reflect two independent factors. First, venue openness: ICLR is OpenReview-native and publishes both accept and reject decisions with full review threads. Its Reject pool is therefore the deepest. ICML and NeurIPS expose decisions on OpenReview only from 2023 onward. ICML 20232024 in particular released very few rejected submissions, which is why those rows show 03 in the Reject column. Second, review accessibility: even when decision is published, the meta-review and reviewer comments may be withheld or formatted such that automatic parsing fails. This is why metadata coverage (Section 3.2) shows only 340 of the 1,947 papers carrying meta-review, even though all 1,947 have decisions. Finally, NeurIPS 2025 contributes no HC papers. As the most recent venue (December 2025), its citation counts had not accumulated enough at collection time for even top-10 cut to be meaningful. These asymmetries do not affect the clustering stage, but we treat them carefully in the quantitative analysis (Section 7).

sections.content: After merging and deduplicating by OpenReview submission id, 1,947 unique papers remain. All 1,947 carry the eight base fields. The four abstracted fields (Section 4) are populated with non-empty content for 1,891 papers. The remaining 56 papers are dropped from the embedding pool (5). Coverage of the initial merged metadata varies by field. In total, 1,074 papers carry the full abstract, 716 carry parsed reviews, 682 carry an extracted introduction, and 340 carry meta-review. For card generation, we run separate review-recovery pass over public OpenReview threads. The resulting card-level review-evidence coverage is reported in 12. Papers without recoverable review text are still included in signature extraction. 9 Table 1: Dataset composition. The label-inclusive Total is 1,996, giving 1,947 unique papers after the 49 OralHC overlaps. Per-class disjoint pools used in Section 7 are 1,014 Oral, 211 HCdisjoint, and 722 Reject. ICLR 2020 is excluded. sections.title: 3.3 Convention for in-paper paper references
sections.content: Throughout this paper, we reference corpus papers by an internal identifier of the form <VENUE> <YEAR> <NNNN>. Examples include ICLR 2022 0094 and NeurIPS 2023 0753. Each identifier resolves uniquely to an OpenReview submission via the openreview id field in data/dataset/all records full.json. After consulting the dataset, paper can be looked up at https://openreview.net/forum?id=<openreview id>. Of the 1,947 corpus papers, approximately 1,088 are referenced at least once in this report. They usually appear as evidence under methodology cards success-condition or failure-mode list. We do not include all corpus papers as bibliography entries. complete corpus bibliography would obscure the second-order related-work citations that constitute Section 2. The prepared dataset is therefore the authoritative bibliographic resolution layer for any paper id appearing in this report. When specific corpus paper is discussed substantively in body text, we provide proper bibliography entry. Examples include the diffusion-ELBO paper [20] = NeurIPS 2023 0753, LP-FT [21] = ICLR 2022 0094, the DP continual-observation paper [16] = ICML 2023 0551, EigenGame [10] = ICLR 2021 0018, and SAM-as-Bayes [32] = ICLR 2023 0331.

sections.title: 4 Two-Stage Innovation-Signature Extraction
sections.content: To convert each paper into form suitable for strategy-level clustering, we extract twelve-field innovation signature. Eight base fields capture the paper in its own terms. Four describe the innovation mechanism, and four record review and outcome signals. second stage rewrites the four mechanism fields into domainagnostic form. Only these four rewritten fields are embedded for clustering. The eight base fields are retained for extraction and later card evidence.

sections.title: 4.1 Stage 1: Eight base fields
sections.content: For Stage 1, we feed Claude Sonnet 4.6 [2] the papers title, available abstract, available introduction, available reviews, and available meta-review. Introductions are extracted from the PDF when recoverable. The model then returns the following structured fields: 1. innovation approach: one sentence describing how this paper innovates as reasoning strategy. 2. key step: the single most critical reasoning step from diagnosis to solution. 3. why non obvious: the cognitive barrier that blocked others from this approach. 4. trigger condition: domain-agnostic situation where this strategy applies, phrased as When X, apply to achieve Z. 5. reviewer praise: three specific points the reviewers praised. 10 6. reviewer concern: three specific reviewer concerns. 7. acceptance signal: one sentence explaining what made the paper Oral / cited / rejected. 8. contribution type: one of {theoretical, methodological, empirical, benchmark, system}. The first four fields are the most strategy-bearing, so Stage 2 rewrites only those. The last four supply review and outcome evidence used later in card construction. Extraction runs under JSON schema that enforces non-empty lists of length 3 for the two review-derived fields. All 1,947 papers pass Stage 1 extraction.

sections.title: 4.2 Stage 2: Four domain-agnostic rewrites
sections.content: A direct embedding of paper descriptions tends to latch onto domain nouns, such as Transformer, diffusion, image, or molecule. This produces topic clusters rather than strategy clusters. We address this failure mode with second Claude Sonnet 4.6 pass. It rewrites the first four base fields into strategy-only equivalents. The prompt instructs the model to replace domain nouns with generic placeholders, adopt imperative phrasing (Audit . . . , Transfer . . . , Design . . . ), preserve the mechanism, and drop application detail. The resulting four fields are: 1. abstract strategy innovation approach 2. abstract key step key step 3. abstract why non obvious why non obvious 4. abstract trigger condition trigger condition representative contrast illustrates the shift. For the paper High Fidelity Speech Synthesis with Adversarial Networks, innovation approach reads They solved domain transfer problem by borrowing the PatchGAN multi-scale discriminator strategy from image GANs and re-instantiating it at multiple temporal windows in audio, adding conditioning signals to close the fidelity gap. The corresponding abstract strategy reads Transfer multi-scale discrimination strategy from one data modality to another by re-instantiating it at the appropriate domain-specific granularity, then add conditioning signals to close the remaining fidelity gap. The generic version is what we feed into the embedding model. The contribution of this abstraction step to strategy-level clustering is quantified in the ablation of 11. All four abstract fields are returned for the full corpus. Of the 1,947 papers, 1,891 carry non-empty content in all four fields. The remaining 56 have at least one empty field and are dropped from the embedding pool in 5.

sections.title: 5.1 Embedding
sections.content: For each paper, we concatenate the four abstracted strategy fields: abstract strategy, abstract key step, abstract why non obvious, and abstract trigger condition. Each field is prefixed as field: content. The average concatenated length is 1,185 characters. After the Stage 2 rewrite of 4, 1,891 of 1,947 papers carry all four fields. We embed these records with OpenAI text-embedding-3-large (3,072 dimensions) and L2-normalize the vectors.

sections.title: 5.2 Clustering
sections.content: reduces We use UMAP [31] the 3,072-dimensional neighbors=15, min dist=0, and seed 42. HDBSCAN [30] then clusters the reduced space with min samples=min cluster size/3 and cluster selection method=eom. We sweep min cluster size {10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40}; Table 2 reports the full sweep. cosine-normalized space to 10 dimensions. Table 2: HDBSCAN sweep on the 1,891-paper embedding. Selected row bolded. min cluster size 10 15 20 25 30 40 31 19 16 12 8 6 nunclustered % unclustered silhouette 47.7 39.7 46.5 48.8 45.1 44. 0.584 0.527 0.566 0.561 0.505 0.474 902 751 879 923 853 849 11 We select min cluster size=10. This setting identifies 31 ideation sub-patterns and gives the strongest silhouette in the sweep (0.584, computed over clustered points). The tradeoff is that 902/1,891 = 47.7% of papers remain unclustered. Coarsening min cluster size does not recover this fraction; it stays in the 4049% range across the sweep. The unclustered points are therefore far from all density peaks, not fine-granularity artifacts. What unclustered actually means (and why we dont call it noise). HDBSCAN normally labels points outside any density peak as noise. We instead use unclustered because the label is geometric, not substantive. It marks where paper sits in the embedding; it does not mean the strategy is weak, idiosyncratic, or outside the framework. The flagged 47.7% are not strategy-empty. All 1,891 embedded papers carry full content in the four abstract * fields, and the unclustered and clustered groups have comparable median lengths. The label reflects embedding geometry at the chosen mcs threshold. paper lands unclustered when its strategy embedding falls between two or three modal clusters, often because it executes several ideation patterns in roughly equal measure. In short, unclustered means between modal clusters, not outside the strategy space. The 15-pattern framework works at the paper level through independent multi-label tagging (6.5), so it covers unclustered and clustered papers equally.

sections.title: 5.3 Cluster inventory
sections.content: Cluster sizes (excluding unclustered) range from 13 (the smallest valid size) to 86, with mean 31.9 and median 22. The five largest clusters are: C02 (n=86): Controlled Diagnostic Construction with One-Variable-Isolated Items, under Design Confound-Isolating Diagnostic. C18 (n=82): Substrate Adaptation Across Modalities for Mature Recipe, under Unify Heterogeneous Inputs into One Space. C06 (n=59): Probabilistic-Objective Unification via Equivalence Proof, under Prove Equivalence to Unify. C14 (n=50): All-Pairs Attention Operator Replacement, under Substitute the Operator or Representation. C05 under Pivot Identifiability-Theorem Assumption, Limiting (n=50): the on Audit and Pivot an Assumption. Each cluster is automatically labeled by an Opus 4.7 pass. The pass reads the clusters eight centroid-nearest representative papers and produces 26 word label plus one-sentence description. second Opus 4.7 pass then produces level-2 disambiguation card for each cluster. The resulting tactic-level patterns are used in 6.3. Each disambiguation card has six panels: 1. tactical pattern: the clusters specific reasoning move at the granularity below the level-1 ideation pattern. 2. Step-by-Step: 5-step abstract structural-move recipe distilled from the clusters accepted examples, with reject-derived boundaries embedded. 3. differentiation within parent: what distinguishes this cluster from its siblings under the same parent. 4. when to pick this one: the situational trigger conditions under which this sub-pattern is the right choice. 5. tactical failure mode: the cluster-specific failure shape that recurs in its Reject papers. 6. Examples: set of paper-agnostic Oral and Reject lessons. No per-paper citations are used. The lessons are abstract distillations of the clusters success and failure shapes, so that Phase 2.2 generation applies the abstract pattern to new gap rather than mimicking specific papers. We use the 31-cluster inventory as raw material for ideation pattern induction in the next section.

sections.title: 5.4 Per-cluster acceptance composition
sections.content: Although the embedding interleaves Oral, HC, and Reject papers across most clusters (Fig. 4b), the cluster-level proportions are non-uniform. Figure 3 shows each clusters Oral / HC / Reject composition, sorted by Oral rate. Six clusters clear the Oral-safe threshold (pO65% among O+R, nO5). One cluster crosses the Reject-warn threshold (pO35% among O+R, nR5). Each cluster below is written as Oral/HC/Reject counts and Oral share among O+R. The flagging is informational. It is surfaced to human artifact readers and the audit layer, but is never used to pre-filter retrieval or generation: Oral-safe: 6 clusters. These tactics, when properly executed, land at the top of the acceptance distribution. The five sharpest by Oral share among O+R are C21 Sequential-Control Re-Casting under Reframe as Solvable Object (13/0/3, 81%), C09 Low-Sensitivity Operator Substitution under Substitute the Operator or Representation (17/0/4, 81%), C16 Symmetry-Group Construction under Encode Structure by Construction 12 (19/3/7, 73%), C27 Intractable-Problem Re-Casting under Reframe as Solvable Object (12/0/5, 71%), and C30 Curvature-Object Substitution under Substitute the Operator or Representation (14/1/6, 70%). Reject-warn: 1 cluster. C13 Relational-Topology Encoded as Structure under Encode Structure by Construction (4/1/12, 25%) is the only cluster whose reject share dominates by the nR5 threshold. Reviewers consistently flag this clusters papers as proposing topology-as-prior architecture without empirically demonstrating that the topology is doing identifiable work over standard alternatives. Mixed: the remaining 24 clusters. These tactics produce both Oral and Reject papers in comparable shares. They are the bulk of the working space, and acceptance hinges on the per-paper success conditions and failure modes characterized in the methodology cards (Section 6.1). The per-cluster labels and full counts are in clustering/extra/cluster composition.csv. These flags are risk signals, not recommendation labels. Oral-safe does not mean preferred; Rejectwarn does not mean forbidden. They are descriptive risk statistics with limited sample sizes. They are also sensitive to year, conference, and topic saturation. Reject-warn clusters rejected papers may have failed on execution rather than tactic choice. We therefore expose these flags to human artifact readers and to the audit layer as informational risk signals. We do not gate retrieval, generation, or recommendation on them. user whose problem genuinely matches Reject-warn clusters tactic can still proceed, but the sub-pattern card informs them of the documented failure modes. Figure 3: Acceptance composition of the 31 clusters, sorted by Oral rate among O+R. Six clusters clear the 65% Oral threshold; one clears the 65% Reject threshold. The remaining 24 are mixed. Cluster labels are colored by risk flag (green = Oral-safe, red = Reject-warn, gray = mixed; deliberately off the bar palette); the threshold uses pO among O+R, so it is shown via label color rather than an x-axis line, because the bars are O/HC/R shares that include HC. 6 Ideation-Pattern Induction Fine-grained ideation sub-patterns answer what specific move did this paper make? They do not answer the higher-level question: what kind of move is that? To induce that level, we ask Claude Opus 4.7 to build Level-1 ideation-pattern taxonomy from the 31 clusters. The prompt imposes four constraints. The taxonomy must contain 618 entries. Each entry must describe reusable reasoning strategy rather than domain or venue. Each entry must include definition, an operational signature, and when-to-apply clause. Finally, every cluster must map to one primary ideation pattern, with an optional secondary pattern. Opus returns 15-ideation pattern taxonomy  (Table 3)  and 31-cluster mapping in single structured call. We make no edits to either. The 15 ideation patterns are: 13 (a) 31 clusters, colored; unclustered in gray. (b) Colored by label (Oral / HC / Reject). Figure 4: 2D UMAP projection of the 1,891-paper embedding. Oral, HC, and Reject papers are interleaved across essentially every cluster: the embedding captures strategy, not acceptance. Table 3: The 15 induced ideation patterns. ncl=fine-grained clusters mapped as primary; Oral / HC / Reject counts are paper-level primary-mapping counts by cluster-level inheritance (989 of the 1,891 embedded papers; the rest are HDBSCAN-unclustered and receive their pattern via the multi-label pass of 6.5). is the unique paper count under cluster-level primary; because 49 papers are both Oral and High-Cited, rows Oral+HC+Reject can exceed n. Figure 5 sizes each wedge by the Oral+HC+Reject sum. Ideation pattern (id) ncl Oral HC Reject the and Pivot Inputs Object Solvable Diagnostic Supervisory Assumption the Operator into One Space or Representation Fixed Generative Component Audit an (assumption audit and pivot) Substitute (architectural operator substitution) Liberate (generative process redesign) Design Confound-Isolating (controlled diagnostic design) Unify Heterogeneous (unify into shared representation) Reframe as (reframe as solvable object) Manufacture (self supervised signal engineering) Encode by (structural prior encoding) Equivalence Prove (algebraic equivalence unification) Decompose for (heterogeneous decomposition) Decompose and (decompose and delegate) Relax Search Discrete (relax discrete search to continuous) Adapt by Conditioning, (adapt via conditioning) Characterize (characterize limit then surpass) Design Property-Targeting Pretext Objective (targeted self supervised objective) Not Retraining Differentiated Construction Continuous Treatment Limit, Structure Delegate Surpass Solvers Signal Unify Then to to to It 6 4 3 1 1 3 3 1 1 1 1 1 1 94 57 41 30 48 26 30 15 19 20 10 9 11 21 22 24 30 17 9 2 14 12 2 0 2 79 181 109 30 25 28 26 23 27 22 12 14 6 6 94 86 82 66 61 59 47 42 18 15 15 No cluster primary (HDBSCAN-unclustered) 532 355 902 14 Each ideation pattern is specified at the level needed for use in the skill, with its full definition, operational signature, and when-to-apply clause given in the per-pattern card. Non-mutually-exclusive operators. The 15 ideation patterns are not mutually exclusive categories. Several pairs naturally overlap. Audit and Pivot an Assumption can overlap with Prove Equivalence to Unify, because an equivalence proof often relaxes load-bearing assumption. Substitute the Operator or Representation can overlap with Encode Structure by Construction, because structured operator both substitutes and encodes. Manufacture the Supervisory Signal can overlap with Design Property-Targeting Pretext Objective, because the latter is structured form of the former. The cluster-level mapping forces one primary pattern per cluster for organization. The papers themselves often execute several operators in composition, and the paper-level multi-label tagging in 6.5 confirms this empirically. The right reading is therefore compositional: the 15 patterns are ideation operators, and paper is represented as composition of operators rather than assigned to one exclusive class. note on small-sample ideation patterns. Three ideation patterns have small per-paper-primary populations under cluster-level inheritance: Adapt by Conditioning, Not Retraining (n=18), Characterize Limit, Then Surpass It (n=15), and Design Property-Targeting Pretext Objective (n=15). These are valid operators because the underlying clusters are statistically distinct in embedding space. They are also low-support operators: their per-pattern acceptance statistics rest on thin evidence and should not be used as strong recommendation priors. The corresponding analytical cards therefore carry an internal confidence: low flag. The runtime prompt cards do not expose raw counts or confidence flags to the model, because doing so induced distribution-bias homogenization toward high-n patterns in earlier designs. Low-support patterns remain available when the users problem genuinely fits them. They are neither privileged nor down-weighted by the skills selection step. The corpus-side flag is retained here for analytical transparency. Plain-language aliases. The ideation pattern names use compact technical phrasing for analytical precision: for example, Audit and Pivot an Assumption and Substitute the Operator or Representation. Each pattern also has plain-language alias for user-facing skill output. This keeps the framework legible to non-specialists. Examples include Audit and Pivot an Assumption Audit the load-bearing assumption and pivot, Reframe as Solvable Object Reformulate the unsolved as solvable object, and Substitute the Operator or Representation Substitute the operator or representation.

sections.title: 6.1 From definition to operational card
sections.content: A definition and operational signature tell researcher what an ideation pattern is. They do not explain when it lands and when it fails. To capture that distinction, we run second Opus 4.7 pass for each ideation pattern. The input includes all Oral, HC, and Reject papers assigned to the pattern, their four abstracted fields, and the available review and meta-review evidence. Review evidence is recovered for 83% of the corpus overall; details are in 12. The pass returns structured card with seven panels: 1. success conditions: cross-Oral patterns of what makes the pattern land. 2. failure modes: cross-Reject patterns of how it fails. 3. oral reject gap: the prose contrast between the two pools. 4. oral hc gap: the prose contrast between PC-favored and community-favored execution. 5. reviewer expectations: bullets tagged by provenance ([oral reviews], [reject reviews], [both]). 6. cognitive barriers: the field-level blind spots that make the pattern non-obvious. 7. representative examples: 68 paper-anchored exemplars (Oral and Reject), each with one-line lesson. This rule applies to success conditions, Evidence-bearing fields must cite paper IDs. failure modes, oral reject gap, oral hc gap, reviewer expectations, and representative examples. The model is also instructed to set confidence=low when sample sizes or review coverage fall below thresholds, and to refuse the Oral-vs-HC contrast when nHC < 5. The 15 resulting cards are the operational substrate of the skill. Definitions, operational signatures, and whento-apply clauses support Phase 2.1 pattern fit. Sub-pattern tactical recipes support Phase 2.2 instantiation. Failure modes, Oral-vs-Reject gaps, representative examples, and reviewer expectations support Phase 3 audit and Phase 4 explanation. They are not used as acceptance-rate priors or ranking signals. The cards Examples panel surfaces paper-agnostic Oral and Reject lessons: bullets distilled from the clusters acceptance pool, without per-paper citations. This design keeps Phase 2.2 focused on applying the abstract pattern to new gap rather than mimicking any single corpus paper. App. reproduces the Audit and Pivot an Assumption cardpaired with one of its sub-pattern cardsas high-coverage corpus-to-card example.

sections.title: 6.2 Coverage and granularity
sections.content: The 15 ideation patterns have two coverage figures, and we report both because they answer different questions: Cluster-level coverage: 989 of 989 clustered papers (100% of clustered; 52% of the 1,891-paper embedding pool) receive primary ideation pattern by inheritance from their ideation sub-pattern. Every cluster maps to exactly one of the 15 primary patterns; there is no out-of-taxonomy residual. Paper-level multi-label coverage: the independent multi-label tagging pass (6.5) assigns one or more ideation pattern labels to all papers including the 902 HDBSCAN-unclustered ones. The 15-ideation pattern framework therefore covers the corpus completely at the paper level, even though only 52% of papers are assignable via cluster-level inheritance. The distribution has long tail. By cluster-level primary count, the top three ideation patterns account for 384 papers: Audit and Pivot an Assumption (n=181), Substitute the Operator or Representation (n=109), and Liberate Fixed Generative Component (n=94). The bottom three account for 48 papers: Adapt by Conditioning (n=18), Characterize Limit (n=15), and Design Property-Targeting Pretext Objective (n=15). This is the long-tail signature of an inductively derived taxonomy. Many innovations use small number of deeply worked strategies; smaller tail uses rare but still coherent operators. Figure 5 visualizes the hierarchy with three concentric rings. The inner ring shows the 15 ideation patterns, sized by paper count. The middle ring subdivides each pattern into its constituent sub-clusters, 31 in total, tinted with the parent patterns color. The outer band encodes each patterns Oral acceptance rate on lightdark gray scale. Two empirical observations follow. (i) Methodology fragmentation is uneven across patterns, but the taxonomy is not over-split. Audit and Pivot an Assumption decomposes into 6 sub-clusters. Substitute the Operator or Representation decomposes into 4. Reframe as Solvable Object, Manufacture the Supervisory Signal, Encode Structure by Construction, and Liberate Fixed Generative Component decompose into 3 each. The remaining 9 patterns are single-cluster patterns, which indicates tactically coherent moves that the abstraction does not split further. The 6-cluster decomposition under Audit and Pivot reflects substantively different audit-pivot moves, such as identifiability-theorem pivots, distributional-assumption pivots, and sufficiency-bound pivots. (ii) Cluster sizes are tight and well-supported. Cluster sizes range from 13 to 86 (mean 31.9, median 22). Of the 31 clusters, only 2 are small (n < 15); the corresponding tactical cards are auto-flagged as confidence: low. The tight size distribution corresponds to high silhouette (0.584).

sections.title: 6.3 Multi-strategy assignments
sections.content: This subsection reports the cluster-level view of multi-strategy composition. This view is deliberately limited: the cluster-level mapping caps each paper at primary + secondary. The deeper paper-level multi-label view in 6.5 recovers the true modal composition, including the 902 HDBSCAN-unclustered papers excluded here. It should be read as the authoritative composition analysis. Paper-level coverage of the secondary slot. The cluster-level (primary, secondary) assignment propagates to papers. Of the 989 clustered papers, 729 (73.7%) carry both primary and non-trivial secondary, while 260 (26.3%) carry only primary. The 902 HDBSCAN-unclustered papers are excluded from these cluster-level statistics and assigned later by multi-label tagging in 6.5. Multi-strategy assignment is therefore the norm among coherently clustered papers. Two cluster-level signals are worth stating because both fall outside the Oral-enriched paper-level ranking in 6.5  (Table 6)  . First, the clearest reject-enriched combination is architectural operator substitution + heterogeneous decomposition (17.9 pp from the 58.4% baseline), followed by algebraic equivalence unification + operator substitution (8.4 pp). The likely failure mode is stacked unresolved tradeoffs: more expressive operator is asked to absorb multi-population heterogeneity, so reviewers read the operator as the architecture rather than as controlled inductive bias. Second, composition itself tracks acceptance. The 729 paired papers reach pO = 58.2% (366/263), essentially the 58.4% baseline. The 260 single-pattern papers reach only 52.7% (116/104, 5.7 pp). single-primary cluster tends to be lone tactical move, whose acceptance signal is weaker than deliberately composed pair. 16 Figure 5: Ideation-pattern hierarchy across 989 clustered papers. Three concentric rings share one angular layout, ordered by size clockwise from 12 oclock; categorical colors identify the 15 patterns and are Inner ring: the 15 induced Level-1 ideation reused across all rings, with white gaps separating segments. patterns, each wedge sized by its clustered-paper count (the count is printed inside the larger wedges; full pattern names are in the right-hand legend). Middle ring: each patterns arc is subdivided into its constituent sub-clusters (31 in total), shaded as lighter tint of the parent pattern; the number of segments within wedge shows how finely that pattern fragments, while the segment angle is the equal share within the pattern, not the per-sub-cluster paper count. Outer ring: thin heat band encoding each patterns Oral acceptance rate pO = nO/(nO + nHC + nR) on the light-to-dark gray scale shown at left (light = low, dark = high), so reviewer outcome can be read against methodology at glance. Totals: 15 patterns, 31 sub-clusters, 989 clustered papers (of 1,891 embedded).

sections.content: Why do certain ideation pattern pairs combine often? The OpenAI text-embedding-3-large embedding gives direct geometric measure. Each ideation pattern has centroid, computed as the mean of its papers normalized embeddings. Pairwise centroid cosine then quantifies how close two patterns are in strategy space. Figure 6 shows the full 15 15 matrix, and Table 4 gives each patterns nearest neighbor. Table 4: Nearest-neighbor ideation pattern and centroid cosine. Sorted by descending cosine. Three observations: (i) Adjacency predicts the Oral-enriched combinations. The top three cluster-level Oral-enriched pairs 17 Figure 6: Centroid-cosine similarity between the 15 ideation patterns. Rows/columns ordered by mean similarity (most central ideation patterns first). All off-diagonal entries fall in [0.79, 0.96]: ideation patterns are not orthogonal in this embedding; they form dense neighborhood on low-dimensional manifold. are all high-cosine neighbors: Audit and Pivot + Structural Prior Encoding (+6.1 pp), Operator Substitution + Audit and Pivot (+5.5 pp), and Audit and Pivot + Controlled Diagnostic Design (+3.7 pp). Audit and Pivot an Assumption sits at cosine 0.949 from Operator Substitution, and connects to Structural Prior Encoding at 0.946. Geometric proximity is therefore useful evidence for which secondary pattern is plausible to compose with given primary. (ii) The two most isolated patterns are not simply the smallest. Design Confound-Isolating Diagnostic (n=86) has the lowest nearest-neighbor cosine (0.904). Characterize Limit, Then Surpass It (n=15) has the second-lowest (0.917). Isolation therefore does not track support alone. The controlled diagnostic pattern is geometrically distinct because its operational signature is to build confound-isolating measurement instrument, which does not compose with the other 14 patterns in standard way. This makes it high-novelty leg candidate. (iii) Three patterns share hub status. Audit and Pivot an Assumption, Reframe as Solvable Object, and Substitute the Operator or Representation each appear as the nearest neighbor for 3 other patterns. The hub set is distributed across diagnosis, framing, and substitution moves. Phase 2.1 therefore has three balanced default secondaries to consider when the bottleneck does not already imply specific composition.

sections.title: 6.5 Paper-level multi-label ideation pattern assignment
sections.content: The cluster-level mapping in 6 caps each cluster, and therefore each clustered paper, at primary + secondary. To test whether 2 is the right cap, and to surface 3+ pattern combinations directly, we run separate Sonnet 4.6 pass on every paper independently. Given the 15 ideation-pattern definitions, the paper title, the abstract, and the four abstract fields, the model emits JSON list of patterns the paper actually executes, not merely mentions. Each label includes quoted evidence span and primary/supporting role. There is no upper cap on = applied patterns; the minimum is 1. 18 All 1,891 papers with the four abstracted fields received non-empty multi-label assignment. Rather than report Bayesian acceptance rate (which requires constructing denominator that mixes the OpenReview Oral/Reject decision with the citation-based HC class), we report the class-conditional distribution (k decision) directly. Table 5 shows, within each acceptance class, what fraction of that classs papers execute ideation patterns. Table 5: Class-conditional distribution. Overall, 59.2% of papers execute exactly two ideation patterns. The mode is shared across Oral, HC, and Reject. Only 7.2% of papers execute single pattern, while 33.6% tail executes three or more. The cluster-based view in 6.3, capped at 2 by construction, therefore under-counts the 3-way tail. Paper-level multi-label tagging is the right granularity for capturing that tail. (ii) Class means cluster tightly around = 2.3. The class means kR = 2.26 kO = 2.28 kHC = 2.32 are statistically indistinguishable at this sample size. Composition size does not separate acceptance classes; composition content (which patterns) does (7). (iii) Cluster-pair assignment under-represents true ideation-pattern coverage. Cross-tabulating against the cluster-derived (primary, secondary) pair shows that multi-label tagging picks up signal beyond the dominant cluster. Most papers have at least one supporting pattern outside their cluster pair. The cluster pair captures the dominant axis, but not the routine 12 supporting moves that multi-label tagging surfaces. Figure 7: Multi-label ideation pattern assignment. (a) distribution as grouped counts by acceptance class. (b) Class-conditional distribution (k decision): each curve is probability mass function summing to 100%. k=2 is the mode for all three classes. Top combinations under multi-label tagging. With paper-level multi-label assignments, we can rank 2-way and 3-way combinations directly  (Table 6)  . We use the same definition of pO = nO/(nO + nR) as in the cluster-level table, so the cross-table comparison is clean; the dataset baseline is pO = 58.4%. Three combinations stand out: Algebraic Equivalence Unification + Generative Process Redesign (nO + nR = 20, pO = 85.0%, +26.6 pp), Algebraic Equivalence Unification + Heterogeneous Decomposition (nO + nR = 22, pO = 81.8%, +23.4 pp), and Audit and Pivot + Characterize Limit, Then Surpass It (nO + nR = 60, pO = 80.0%, +21.6 pp). The third is the highest-volume strongly Oral-enriched combination in the dataset. The skill treats ideation-pattern composition as 13 entries per generated idea, with = 2 as the empirically supported default. For each composed pattern, it draws success and failure conditions from the ideation-pattern cards in 6.1. sections.title: 7 Acceptance and Impact Analysis
sections.content: We define acceptance bias as OR(m) = poral(m) preject(m). Here, poral(m) is the fraction of cluster-level Oral primaries whose primary pattern is m, and preject(m) is the analogous fraction for Reject papers. Positive OR means the pattern appears more often in accepted work than in rejected work, relative to class totals. Negative OR means the opposite. This analysis is cluster-level and therefore partial. cluster-level primary is available for 482 of 1,014 Oral papers and 367 of 722 Reject papers. The denominators of pX (m) are the cluster-level totals (NO = 482, NHC = 172, NR = 367), not the full class populations. The remaining HDBSCAN-unclustered papers are covered by the multi-label tagging pass in 6.5. Figure 9 plots the bias for all 15 patterns; Table 7 reports all values. OR=poral preject in percentage points. OH=poral phc (positive = PC-favored over community); reported only when nHC 5.
