Two axes structure the table. The Oral-vs-Reject axis OR is tight, with spread of only 2.9 pp. At the main-pattern level, strategy choice contributes little to acceptance. The orthogonal PC-vs-community axis OH is much wider, reaching roughly 13 pp, and is the stronger discriminating signal. Four observations follow. (O1) The OR axis is tight. The range is 2.9 pp: Reframe as Solvable Object is at +2.9, while Decompose for Differentiated Treatment is at 2.9. This narrow spread suggests that the main pattern alone explains little acceptance variance. Execution at the sub-pattern level, meaning the cluster rather than the Level-1 pattern, is what discriminates. (O2) Audit and Pivot is high-volume, high-variance, slightly Reject-leaning, and heavily PCfavored. It carries 19.5% of Oral papers, the largest cluster-level Oral share, and 21.5% of Reject papers, also the largest. This gives OR=2.0 pp. Its OH=+13.1 pp is the largest positive PC bias in the taxonomy: PCs disproportionately Oral audit/pivot papers, while the community cites them less than expected. Execution quality, not strategy choice, separates this patterns 94 Oral from 79 Reject papers. Figure 9: Per-ideation-pattern acceptance bias. Bars show OR; positive bars are Oral-enriched and negative bars are Reject-enriched. Diamonds show OH where nHC 5. (O3) Unify Heterogeneous Inputs into One Space is the datasets clearest community-favored pattern. Its OH=11.2 pp is driven by 17.4% HC share against 6.2% Oral share, proportionally almost three times more HC than Oral. Cross-modal-alignment papers are reproduced and cited heavily, but PCs do not elevate them to Oral at the same rate. This is the inverse of Audit and Pivot. (O4) Reframe as Solvable Object is the cleanest Oral signal. Both OR=+2.9 pp and OH=+7.1 pp are positive. PCs reward the pattern, and the community does not over-cite it relative to Oral frequency. Combined with its high cosine to Audit and Pivot (0.956) in the adjacency analysis, this supports concrete Oral recipe: recast an intractable native formulation as solvable object, then audit and pivot the assumption that the recast makes load-bearing. The PC-vs-community axis (OH). The OH=poral phc column isolates second axis: whether program committees and the citing community agree on what counts. Observations O2O4 show that PCs tend to reward structural-insight moves, while the community tends to reward usable-infrastructure moves. The two rankings are nearly orthogonal. The audit layer therefore surfaces OH as distinct signal, separate from the acceptanceprediction axis. IdeaSpark does not use the acceptance or PC-vs-community statistics as selection priors at Use in the skill. any phase. Pattern selection is driven by structural fit to the bottleneck. The statistics enter only as context for audit.

sections.title: 8 Domain Distribution and Ideation-Pattern Breadth
sections.content: The ideation-pattern cards (6.1) and the per-pattern bias profile (7) treat the 1,947 papers as one population. ML, however, is not monolithic field. strategy that lands an Oral in learning theory may not land in visionIdeaSpark therefore needs to distinguish broadly transferable patterns from domainlanguage pretraining. conditional ones. This section quantifies that distinction.

sections.title: 8.1 Domain induction
sections.content: We do not preset domain taxonomy. Instead, we induce one from the data using the same architecture that produced the 15 ideation patterns: 22 1. Sonnet 4.6 extracts 13 normalized research-subarea tags per paper from its title, abstract, and (when available, 77.9% of the dataset) author-supplied OpenReview keywords. 2. The 3,909 unique free-form tags are embedded with OpenAI text-embedding-3-large and clustered with UMAP+HDBSCAN (k=148 tag-clusters at mcs=7, unclustered 25.5%). 3. Sonnet 4.6 labels each tag-cluster. 4. Opus 4.7 induces Level-1 domain taxonomy of [8, 30] entries and maps each tag-cluster to domain label. The model returns =28 domains. We make no edits. The per-paper assignment is the union of domains over each papers tags. Coverage is high: 98.5% of papers (1,918 / 1,947) receive at least one domain. The average paper receives 1.81 domains. The 28 domains range from n=256 (Transfer, Continual & Meta-Learning) to n=16 (Online & Bandit Learning). The full ordered list and machine-readable taxonomy are in clustering/domains/domain taxonomy.json.

sections.title: 8.2 Domain × ideation pattern heatmap
sections.content: We cross the 28 induced domains with the 15 ideation patterns using paper-level multi-label tagging (6.5). Each paper contributes one count to every (domain, pattern) cell it belongs to. The grid has 28 15 = 420 cells. Of these, 392 (93%) contain at least one paper, and 292 (70%) have nO + nR 5. Figure 10 shows two views. Panel (a) reports raw paper count per cell. Panel (b) reports the cell-level Oral rate pO = nO/(nO + nR), masked where nO + nR < 5. Figure 10: (a) Paper counts per (domain, ideation pattern) cell, with domains and ideation patterns sorted by total. (b) Per-cell Oral rate pO (deeper green is higher; gray cells have nO + nR < 5, too few papers to estimate rate; the colorbar marks the 58.4% dataset average). The colored signal in (b) is heterogeneous: the same ideation pattern lands at very different Oral rates across domains. The cell-level view adds domain specificity to 7. The same ideation pattern can land at very different Oral rates across domains. One pattern is the main exception: Characterize Limit, Then Surpass It gives the cleanest cross-domain Oral signal in the corpus, occupying four of the five strongest cells. The five strongest cells, each with pO 90% on nO + nR 8, are: Trustworthy & Responsible ML Characterize Limit, Then Surpass It: 100% Oral (nO=12, nR=0), the datasets cleanest acceptance cell. Learning Theory & Generalization Characterize Limit, Then Surpass It: 100% (11/0). Reinforcement Learning Design Confound-Isolating Diagnostic: 93% (13/1). Representation & Self-Supervised Learning Characterize Limit, Then Surpass It: 91% (10/1). 23 Graph Learning Characterize Limit, Then Surpass It: 91% (10/1). The five weakest dense cells (pO 25%) are: Distributed & Federated Learning Reframe as Solvable Object: 9%. Graph Learning Substitute the Operator or Representation: 21%. Visual Perception & Recognition Decompose for Differentiated Treatment: 22%. Model Efficiency & Compression Adapt by Conditioning, Not Retraining: 25%. Graph Learning Liberate Fixed Generative Component: 25%. The weakest dense cells show the other side of the same result. pattern that wins in one domain can under-perform sharply in another. This supports the cards guidance that success conditions do not transfer mechanically across domains. The cross-domain consistency of Characterize Limit, Then Surpass It has clear operational basis. When paper identifies formal limit, such as an information-theoretic, sample-complexity, expressivity, or generalization limit, and proves strict improvement over it, the reviewer-defensible signature transfers across otherwise different domains. 8.3 Ideation-pattern breadth: how domain-agnostic is each pattern? For the skill to judge whether pattern is plausible in users area, it needs each patterns domain footprint. Figure 11 plots, for each ideation pattern, the number of domains it touches and the share of its papers concentrated in its largest domain. Figure 11: Ideation-pattern breadth: number of distinct domains each ideation pattern has 5 papers in (out of 28). The annotation gives each patterns multi-label paper count: paper is counted under every pattern it carries, so these counts overlap across patterns and sum to more than the 1,891-paper corpus. Most patterns touch 18 domains, supporting the skills domain-agnostic positioning. Three observations: (i) Diagnostic and framing patterns reach the entire field. Audit and Pivot an Assumption and Reframe as Solvable Object each have 5 papers in all 28 domains. Decompose for Differentiated Treatment reaches 27 domains. These three are also the highest-volume patterns in the multi-label distribution, with 1008, 527, 24 and 529 papers respectively under tagging. They also anchor the Oral-enriched multi-label combinations in Table 6. Breadth, volume, and acceptance signal therefore co-occur. (ii) Construction-level patterns are still broad, but more concentrated. Encode Structure by Construction reaches 25 domains, Liberate Fixed Generative Component reaches 25, Manufacture the Supervisory Signal reaches 23, and Substitute the Operator or Representation reaches 22. These patterns depend on specific architectural choice or generative inductive bias. They appear across most of the field, but with clearer domain-modal anchors. Generative redesign concentrates in Diffusion, Flow & Score Models, and operator substitution concentrates in Sequence Modeling & Architectures. (iii) Domain-narrow patterns are not necessarily Reject-prone. Relax Discrete Search to Continuous touches 9 domains, Unify Heterogeneous Inputs into One Space touches 11, and Characterize Limit, Then Surpass It touches 12. The first two are narrow because their operational substrates exist only in some subfields: discrete-search-amenable problems and cross-modal alignment scaffolds. Characterize Limit, Then Surpass It is also narrow, but it is the cleanest cross-domain Oral signal we observe (8.2). Its narrowness reflects prerequisite. The field must already have produced formal limit characterization, which is not true everywhere. We do not report per-domain temporal slice. Conditioning on (domain year pattern) leaves only 13 papers per cell for smaller domains, which is too thin for quantitative claim. The skill also does not rely on such aggregate slices. Its Phase 2.1 saturation read is computed live from each runs Phase 0 retrieval (9), so domain-specific saturation is read from the actual retrieved literature rather than from coarse dataset bins.

sections.title: 9.1 Temporal trends
sections.content: Figure 12: Per-pattern share of papers by year under multi-label tagging (% of that years papers carrying the pattern in any role; rows sum to 230%). Left: top-8 by total volume. Right: remaining seven. Lines that rise through the window are the patterns currently ascending; those that fall are losing share. We summarize temporal change across the 20212025 window by comparing an early period, defined as the average over 20212022, with late period, defined as the average over 20242025. The clearest movers are: Risers. Decompose and Delegate to Solvers (0.0% early 6.3% late, = +6.3 pp) is the datasets fastestgrowing pattern. It tracks the emergence of LLM-driven multi-agent and tool-orchestrated solver pipelines from 2023 onward. Design Confound-Isolating Diagnostic (11.3% 15.6%, = +4.3 pp) reflects the maturation of controlled-perturbation evaluation as first-class research move. Unify Heterogeneous Inputs into One Space ( = +2.6 pp) is the third clearly rising pattern. Fallers. Decompose for Differentiated Treatment (34.7% 26.2%, = 8.5 pp) loses the most ground. The split into sub-populations and treat differently move appears to have been absorbed into more specialized patterns, including operator substitution and structural priors. Audit and Pivot an Assumption (58.7% 50.9%, = 7.8 pp) remains the highest-volume pattern, but gradually gives back share. Liberate Fixed Generative Component ( = 5.7 pp) is the third faller, consistent with saturation of the generative-redesign wave that dominated 20212023. 25 Sample-size caveat for early years. The corpus is unbalanced across years. The 2021 slice has 77 multilabeled papers and 2022 has 73, while each year from 2023 to 2025 has 537606. The early-period share is therefore aggregated over 150-paper base. Changes at the lower end of the table ( < 2 pp) are not statistically robust, so we do not call them out. The risers and fallers above all carry 2.5 pp, corresponding to absolute paper-count moves of more than 15 papers per direction. How the skill uses these trend signals. These corpus-wide temporal verdicts are descriptive context only. The skill never loads them as generation priors. Instead, Phase 1 reads pattern saturation live from each runs own retrieved literature (13).

sections.title: 9.2 Conference preferences
sections.content: Figure 13 reports each patterns share within each venue, measured as the percentage of that venues papers carrying the pattern under multi-label tagging. Three venue signatures emerge. Figure 13: Pattern usage by venue under multi-label tagging. Each bar reports the share of papers in venue that carry the pattern in any role. Patterns are sorted by total volume. ICML over-indexes on assumption-audit and limit-characterization. Audit and Pivot an Assumption reaches 56.2% share at ICML versus 52.4% elsewhere, +3.8 pp lift. Characterize Limit, Then Surpass It reaches 5.7% versus 4.3%, +1.4 pp lift. Both patterns are diagnostic or theoretical, consistent with ICMLs preference for sharply bounded analytical contributions. ICLR over-indexes on construction-side moves. Encode Structure by Construction reaches 20.6% versus 16.2% elsewhere (+4.4 pp). Liberate Fixed Generative Component reaches 16.2% versus 13.3% (+3.0 pp). ICLR also under-indexes on Prove Equivalence to Unify (2.7 pp) and Audit and Pivot an Assumption (4.5 pp). The venue signature favors architectural and representational construction over algebraic unification. NeurIPS over-indexes on heterogeneity-driven decomposition and supervisory-signal engineering. Decompose for Differentiated Treatment reaches 31.4% versus 26.8% elsewhere (+4.7 pp). Manufacture the Supervisory Signal reaches 18.0% versus 14.3% (+3.7 pp). NeurIPS under-indexes on Design Confound-Isolating Diagnostic (4.0 pp). The venue signature is broadly systems-level composition plus invented supervision, consistent with its empirical bias toward large-experiment papers. These venue signatures are quantified per pattern. The skill surfaces them only as audit context for the Phase 4 idea card, so the user can see the venue prior alongside the candidates chosen pattern composition.

sections.title: 10 Do Rejected Papers Inhabit a Different Strategy Space?
sections.content: A natural concern is that clustering Oral, HC, and Reject papers in one space may force Reject papers into clusters defined by accepted work. If rejected papers used different strategies, the induced ideation patterns would under-represent them. We test this directly. The result is negative: rejected work clusters within the same 15-pattern taxonomy rather than in strategy space of its own.

sections.title: 10.1 Reject-only clustering and mapping
sections.content: We re-run the embedding+clustering pipeline on the 711 Reject papers that carry full abstract * fields. Of the 722 Reject papers, 11 lack one of the four fields. We keep the UMAP hyperparameters fixed (n components=5, neighbors=10, cosine metric, seed 42). We also keep HDBSCAN fixed at min cluster size=10 with cluster selection method=leaf. This configuration produces 13 Reject-only clusters, with an unclustered fraction of 40.5% (288/711). We then map each Reject-only cluster to its nearest main-pattern centroid in the original 3,072-dimensional text-embedding-3-large space (6). All 13 clusters land within 0.8970.986 cosine of their assigned primary. In total, 11 of the 15 main patterns receive at least one Reject-only cluster. No Reject-only cluster requires an out-of-taxonomy label. Four main patterns receive no Reject-only mapping: Adapt by Conditioning, Not Retraining, Characterize Limit, Then Surpass It, Design Property-Targeting Pretext Objective, and Encode Structure by Construction. Three are small-sample patterns (n 18 each), and one is constructively encoded pattern. The empirical reading is that Reject papers do not form separate strategic space. They map into the existing 15-pattern taxonomy at high cosine. Table 8 compares the resulting Reject distribution against the main-taxonomy Reject distribution. Table 8: Reject distribution. The Reject-only column counts papers in each main pattern via Reject-onlyclustering nearest-main-centroid mapping; the Reject-in-full column counts the same papers via their main-cluster primary inheritance. is the divergence ( 0: Reject-only assigns more papers here than the main taxonomy does). sections.title: 10.2 Interpretation
sections.content: Ten of the 15 patterns yield comparable Reject counts across the two computations ( 10). The Reject pools strategic shape is therefore stable whether we cluster it alongside accepted papers or separately. 27 The notable divergences are concentrated in two directions. Reject-only clustering assigns substantially more papers to Unify Heterogeneous Inputs into One Space (+52) and Audit and Pivot an Assumption (+19) than the in-full mapping does. Conversely, it assigns fewer papers to Encode Structure by Construction (23), Reframe as Solvable Object (15), and Manufacture the Supervisory Signal (10). The pattern is consistent: when Reject papers cluster among themselves, they move toward the audit/unify lanes and away from constructive lanes. The likely interpretation is that failed constructive moves can appear structurally like audit/unify moves once stripped of the construction that made them constructive. Figure 14: Left: Reject-only clustering (n=711, 13 clusters, 40.5% unclustered). Right: Reject-only-clustering main mapping vs. main taxonomy primary distribution over the same Reject papers. The two columns largely agree; the divergences are concentrated on Unify Heterogeneous Inputs into One Space and Audit and Pivot an Assumption (over-attracted) vs. Encode Structure by Construction (absent from the Reject-only clustering). Positive. The 15-pattern taxonomy covers the Reject population without modification. Every Reject-only cluster maps into an existing pattern, with no out-of-taxonomy bucket. Rejection is therefore not explained by use of different kind of strategy. Negative. The Reject-only divergence ((cid:80) = 198) shows that the fine-grained surface form of rejected work drifts toward more general audit/unify patterns and away from constructive patterns. This has direct consequence for the skill. When Phase 2.1 selects constructive primary pattern, such as Encode Structure by Construction, Reframe as Solvable Object, or Manufacture the Supervisory Signal, Phase 3.2 should check whether the candidates executed surface form has collapsed toward the corresponding audit/unify lane.

sections.title: 11 Ablation: Embedding Model and Abstraction Stage
sections.content: Two design choices are central to the induced taxonomy. First, the production pipeline uses general-purpose paraphrase-strong encoder rather than SPECTER2. Second, it embeds the domain-agnostic rewrites rather than the four original base fields. We ablate both choices on the same 1,891-paper subset used by the production pipeline. UMAP parameters are held fixed, and HDBSCAN is swept over min cluster size {10, 15, 20, 25}. Figure 15 and Table 9 report the comparison.

sections.title: 11.1 Embedding model: OpenAI vs SPECTER2
sections.content: With identical input text and identical downstream processing, OpenAI text-embedding-3-large produces more clusters than SPECTER2 at min cluster size = 10 (31 vs. 23). It also achieves substantially higher silhouette (0.584 vs. 0.438, +0.15 gap). SPECTER2 degrades across the sweep, reaching 0.333 at min cluster size = 25. Its unclustered fraction remains in the 4154% range. The qualitative difference matches the training objectives. SPECTER2 is trained on citation-informed contrastive pairs and is strong at placing topically adjacent papers near each other. Our target, however, is strategy similarity rather than topic similarity. Two papers should cluster together if they both re-instantiate proven mechanism at the appropriate domain-specific granularity, even if they work on different modali28 Figure 15: Clustering quality across three embedding-input choices, as min cluster size varies. Solid line: silhouette. Dashed line: number of clusters k. Production (left) is stable. OpenAI + base fields (middle) collapses to = 2 degenerate mega-clusters at min cluster size = 1520. SPECTER2 (right) stays at lower silhouette across the sweep. Table 9: Clustering ablation. All three variants use UMAP (10-dim, neighbors=15, min dist=0, cosine) and an HDBSCAN sweep over min cluster size. The two OpenAI rows differ only in which text is embedded. The production configuration is bolded. Embedding Text mcs Unclustered % Silhouette OpenAI text-embedding-3-large OpenAI text-embedding-3-large OpenAI text-embedding-3-large 4 abstract fields 4 abstract fields 4 abstract fields OpenAI text-embedding-3-large OpenAI + base (degenerate at mcs=15) OpenAI + base (degenerate at mcs=20) 4 base fields 4 base fields 4 base fields SPECTER2 (768-dim) SPECTER2 (768-dim) 4 abstract fields 4 abstract fields 10 15 25 10 15 20 10 31 19 12 35 2 2 23 7 47.7 39.7 48.8 35.7 0.0 0.0 50.6 41. 0.584 0.527 0.561 0.586 0.884 0.884 0.438 0.333 ties. The general-purpose paraphrase encoder better preserves this strategy-equivalence relation and produces tighter strategy partition.

sections.title: 11.2 Abstraction stage: silhouette is not enough
sections.content: The base-fields ablation gives more subtle result. At min cluster size = 10, its silhouette is nearly identical to production (0.586 vs. 0.584). Its unclustered fraction is also lower (35.7% vs. 47.7%). On the raw clustering metric, the base-fields variant therefore looks slightly better. The same configuration is unstable across the sweep. At min cluster size = 1520, it collapses to = 2 megaclusters with silhouette 0.884 and 0% unclustered. Inspection shows that these are not strategy clusters. One aggregates papers about engineering choices in deep learning. The other aggregates papers about formal or analytic ML. They are macro-topic clusters, not reasoning-move clusters. This degenerate collapse indicates that topic cohesion has overpowered strategy cohesion. The base fields contain domain-specific vocabulary, including architecture names, dataset names, and field-specific mathematical objects. At coarser granularities, topic proximity then dominates the similarity structure. Two diffusion papers can cluster together because both are about diffusion, regardless of what they actually do with diffusion. The Stage-2 rewrite moves abstraction into the text before embedding. It removes topic anchors and leaves strategy-level signal. The production configuration remains well behaved across coarser settings: stays between 12 and 19, and silhouette stays in the 0.530.58 range. It does not collapse into mega-clusters. Ablation takeaways. We select the production configuration for construct validity and stability, not because it maximizes silhouette. The two ablations point in the same direction. The general-purpose paraphrase encoder beats SPECTER2 ( silhouette = +0.15) because SPECTER2s citation prior anchors on topic rather than on the strategy similarity needed here. The domain-agnostic rewrites tie the base fields on silhouette at the chosen setting, but avoid collapse at adjacent settings. The base-field variant pays for cohesion in the wrong currency: it can achieve high silhouette by forming topic clusters. The Stage-2 rewrite removes those topic anchors and preserves robustness across the granularity sweep without giving up cluster quality. Production is therefore the only configuration whose clusters support the ideation-pattern taxonomy of 6 while remaining stable across the HDBSCAN sweep.

sections.title: 12.1 Empirical takeaways
sections.content: The analyses above support four claims. fifth claim is supported only at the idea-stage endpoint and remains open for human validation. (i) The strategy space is shared, not stratified. Re-clustering the Reject pool in isolation (10) reproduced the same 15-pattern vocabulary rather than separate space. The signal separating accepted from rejected work therefore lies less in strategy choice than in execution. The per-pattern success conditions and failure modes carry that distinction. The one systematic divergence is that isolated Reject clusters drift toward audit/unify lanes and away from constructive lanes. This suggests that failed constructive moves can look like audit/unify moves once stripped of the construction that defines them. (ii) The Oral-vs-Reject axis is flat at the main-pattern level; PC-vs-community is the stronger discriminator. Across granularities (7), which patterns contributes only small main effect to acceptance. How well they compose contributes larger one. This is the gap targeted by the skills audit step. The nearly orthogonal OH axis, contrasting PC-favored and community-favored patterns, is wider and acts as distinct second signal. The audit surfaces this signal independently of acceptance prediction. This is the softer of the two axes, however: HC papers come from separately curated high-citation set rather than from OpenReview decisions, so we report OH only at nHC 5 and discount it elsewhere, and reconstructing HC from decision-aligned citation percentiles would let it carry the same weight as Oral-vs-Reject. (iii) Methodologies are broad in coverage but concentrated in effect. Most patterns reach the majority of the 28 induced domains (8.3). Yet the same pattern lands at very different Oral rates across domains (Fig. 10b). The cleanest cross-domain Oral signal is Characterize Limit, Then Surpass It. For the skill, this means domain-cell rates should be surfaced as audit context, not loaded as generation priors. (iv) Composition is the norm, with k=2 as the empirical mode. Paper-level multi-label tagging (6.5) shows that composition size does not separate acceptance classes: 2.3 in all three. Composition content matters more. The skill therefore composes one to three patterns selected by bottleneck fit, defaulting to two, rather than ranking patterns by acceptance priors. (v) Partially closed: execution validity at the skill endpoint. The construct-validity analysis in 11 shows that the clusterpatterncard chain is internally coherent and stable across the HDBSCAN sweep. The reject-only analysis shows that every Reject-only cluster maps into the taxonomy. Section 14 adds an endpoint evaluation: when invoked at inference time and judged blind by two automated skills, IdeaSpark ideas receive the highest quality scores among four sources. What remains open is human-grounded validation against expert reviewers, implementation outcomes, and acceptance outcomes. From taxonomy to system. The taxonomy is the prerequisite for the skill system, but it is also standalone deliverable. It has two immediate uses. researcher can compare candidate idea against the 15 ideation patterns and ask which success conditions the draft satisfies. reviewer can also name specific failure mode, for example combination indistinguishable from sum of parts. The skill system in 13 operationalizes both uses inside an LLM scaffold.

sections.title: IdeaSpark Design
sections.content: We build IdeaSpark to turn the empirical substrate in Sections 610 into runnable research-ideation workflow. This section describes its runtime boundaries, evidence flow, phase contracts, audit logic, output surface, and mechanical validators. Endpoint idea quality is evaluated separately by automated judges in Section 14. Humanrated quality and held-out rediscovery remain future work.

sections.title: 13.1 Positioning and two-tier architecture
sections.content: The skill converts an under-specified research direction into one reviewer-facing proposal. successful run returns an artifact bundle centered on compact idea card. The card contains the motivation, method flow, core claim, falsification prediction, and feasibility validation. One user input produces one of three outcomes: an idea-card bundle, do not generate diagnosis when the direction cannot be grounded, or phase 3 failed report when the audit abandons candidate. The skill never asks the user mid-flow. Missing context is inferred when reasonable; unrecoverable ambiguity becomes an explicit stop condition. The skill is not an ideation-pattern recommender, experiment planner, or paper writer. It does not propose datasets, baselines, ablation matrices, expected figures, or calendar plan. Those choices depend on user context the skill cannot access. It produces an idea plus an honest feasibility judgment; empirical engineering remains the users responsibility. IdeaSpark is implemented as two-tier skill. The runtime tier contains compact instructions, orchestration logic, retrieval hooks, phase prompts, rendering scripts, and deterministic validators. The evidence tier contains the 15 ideation-pattern cards, 31 sub-pattern cards, domain-by-pattern matrix, saturation records, and corpusderived failure-mode inventory. Evidence is read progressively. Phase 2 reads only the pattern definitions and sub-pattern tactics needed to instantiate candidate. Phase 3 reads failure modes, anti-pattern context, and collision evidence for audit. Phase 4 reads the audit findings to explain the final card. Raw counts and confidence flags remain analytical metadata for readers. They are not used as generation priors.

sections.title: 13.2 Design principles
sections.content: The skills core stance is that publishable research idea is constructed, not invented from scratch. The reasoning chain has three steps. First, read recent literature in the users area and identify structural gap no retrieved paper closes: the bottleneck. Second, use the induced ideation-pattern vocabulary to ask what kind of research move could close that gap. Third, instantiate the chosen move as concrete mechanism. The 15-pattern and 31-sub-pattern substrate supplies the vocabulary for the second step. It keeps generation at the level of pattern-applied-to-gap rather than unconstrained brainstorming. idea third. Bottleneck identification must be Principle 1: Literature first, vocabulary second, grounded in retrieved papers, not model recall. Pattern selection reads the shape of the unresolved gap and asks which operational move would close it. Idea instantiation then uses the selected sub-pattern as tactical guide. It applies the abstract move to the specific gap rather than copying paper-specific template. Principle 2: Substantive contribution over methodological labels. candidate cannot defend itself as we use ideation pattern while prior work used pattern Y. Method choice is process. The contribution is what the candidate derives, constructs, measures, or tightens that prior work did not. This is enforced in the differentiation deltas and downstream audit checks. The 15 patterns are diagnostic vocabulary, never the contribution claim itself. Principle 3: Generation freedom, audit anchoring. Earlier iterations placed too many corpus-derived constraints inside generation. That biased candidates toward frequent corpus moves and away from the users bottleneck. The current design keeps positive pattern vocabulary in Phase 2, but moves negative and frequencybased signals to audit and explanation. Phase 3 checks whether the candidate survives known failure modes. It does not reject candidate merely because its pattern is common. These choices follow from the empirical substrate. Rejected papers often use the same strategy operators as accepted papers, so the taxonomy serves as thinking and audit vocabulary rather than as hard generator filter. Because = 2 is the modal paper-level composition, with 33.6% tail at 3, Phase 2 allows one to three gap-closure entries. Because some two-way combinations are reject-enriched unless the downstream artifact is substantively delivered, Phase 3 includes an anti-pattern substantive-verification check. Because acceptance is domain-conditional, saturation and domain-landscape information are surfaced as audit and explanation context rather than as recommendation priors.

sections.title: 13.3 Workflow and phase contracts
sections.content: The skill treats the 15 induced ideation patterns as diagnostic vocabulary, not as labels candidate must classify into. The advance path combines model-reasoning phases with deterministic retrieval, rendering, and 31 validation steps. The revise path adds one bounded repair step. That repair is constrained by audit targets and by byte-level preservation of the candidates kill-switch commitments.

sections.title: 13.3.1 Phase 0: Literature grounding
sections.content: Phase 0 builds the broad literature-grounding bundle. It is the only phase that retrieves area-level context. The later Phase 3.1 retrieval is narrower: it checks for mechanism-level collision with the generated candidate. The system turns the user direction into several search queries, checks which connectors are available, and queries four sources. Each source is used for the role it covers best. arXiv supplies recent preprints. OpenReview supplies forward-looking, in-review signal. OpenAlex supplies the broad published-paper graph. Semantic Scholar supplies CS-focused metadata and short summaries. arXiv and OpenReview cover the recent 06 month window. OpenAlex and Semantic Scholar cover the 624 month window. This split is deliberate. No single source provides all four strengths: preprint recency, in-review signal, breadth, and clean cross-identifiers for deduplication. Each source is queried where it is strongest. The two time windows are kept non-overlapping. paper that appears both as recent preprint and as an in-review submission is counted once, avoiding inflated pattern-saturation counts in Phase 1. The retrieved papers are then deduplicated across sources and lightly tagged with ideation patterns for later analysis. Immediately after the literature table is written, Phase 0+ fetches small full-text cache for the most relevant references. Phase 1 hard-gates on this cache, so the pipeline cannot silently degrade to abstract-only bottleneck reasoning. Two refinements harden the retrieval. For recall, keyword matching is complemented by meaning-based pass that finds conceptually close papers with little surface-word overlap. This pass is gated to the home field of the keyword results, so broader matching does not pull in off-field papers. Terminology drift would otherwise hide close competitors. For reading fidelity, the system fetches open-access full text where available. Bottleneck and differentiation judgments can then rest on method sections, not abstracts alone. The overall goal is that every downstream check (bottleneck, citation, collision) traces to retrieved record rather than to model memory. 13.3.2 Phase 1: Bottleneck identification Phase 1 first checks that Phase 0 produced valid grounding bundle and that Phase 0+ produced the full-text cache. It then reads the user direction, retrieved records, abstracts, and cached full-text snippets. Its main output is one literature-grounded bottleneck: concrete structural gap, not topic label. It also names the closest adjacent papers, explains what each still leaves open, lists gaps Phase 0 did not close, and summarizes pattern usage across on-topic papers. These usage bands are descriptive, not temporal. pattern is saturated when it dominates the neighborhood, untested when it appears at most once, and mid-frequency otherwise. The summary is carried forward as audit and explanation context only. To compare gaps on common footing, Phase 1 arranges the neighborhood into method-lineage tree. Each retrieved method is node linked to the earlier method it refines or replaces. The tree runs from foundational ancestors to current leaves. When the recent retrieval window misses an older ancestor, the model may add it as an awareness-only node, explicitly marked non-citable. This division is deliberate. The window is recent because frontier grounding matters most there, and model memory is least reliable there. Older foundational methods are more stable. Because awareness-only nodes make no novelty claim, they serve as regression guards rather than evidence. An anti-fabrication rule keeps the tree shallow on genuinely young lines instead of padding it with invented ancestors. The tree exposes three things flat abstract list cannot. Additive gaps sit at leaf: an unmet need no current method achieves. Subtractive gaps are read from shared ancestor: the load-bearing assumption every leaf inherits. These gaps matter because strong papers often remove an assumed-necessary mechanism rather than add another component. Third, the tree blocks regression. proposed fix that an ancestor already implemented is treated as superseded, not novel. Throughout, the reading is evidence-gated. subtractive opening may name only an assumption the retrieved methods actually show. The route out of Phase 1 is binary: proceed, or stop with diagnostic. Phase 1 never asks the user for missing information. It infers reasonable defaults, for example single-researcher cloud compute budget when none is stated. It stops only when the direction is too broad, has no literature anchor, or the retrieved pool is too sparse to ground bottleneck. The two structural routes cover an estimated 7585% of Oral types in the corpus (theory-plus-reframing, scaling-law, surgical-fix-plus-theory, evaluation-validity-audit, and most empirical-reveal Orals); benchmark-construction and pure system or infrastructure directions are not yet separate triggers and currently resolve through one of the two routes, with the subtype named only as diagnostic explanation. Adding dedicated triggers for them is localized extension that would raise coverage without changing the architecture.

sections.title: 13.3.3 Phase 2: Pattern-guided ideation
sections.content: Phase 2 separates pattern selection from candidate generation. This separation is important: the first step asks which corpus-derived research move can close the diagnosed gap, while the second step instantiates that move as concrete candidate. Pattern selection. The system chooses one anchor gap from Phase 1 and keeps only the sibling gaps that can plausibly form single paper with it. For each retained gap, it reads the 15 main ideation-pattern cards and selects the pattern whose operational signature structurally closes that gap. Pattern frequency and saturation are recorded as audit context, but they do not act as generation priors. Candidate generation. For each selected main pattern, the system chooses one sub-pattern and reads its tactical panels as guide. The output is not just title or theme. It contains the core mechanism, per-gap closure rationale, differentiation from the closest adjacent work, compute budget, and falsification prediction naming what should change if the mechanism is real. Finally, the candidate passes through deterministic sub-pattern citation gate. The gate verifies that every cited sub-pattern exists under the stated parent pattern before any expensive collision retrieval or audit is run.

sections.title: 13.3.4 Phase 3: Quality gauntlet
sections.content: Phase 3 has three steps: focused literature search, corpus-anchored audit, and an optional revision. First, the system re-queries the literature sources using terms drawn from the candidates mechanism and claim. This search is separate from Phase 0 because it looks for recent mechanism overlap, not broad area context. Second, the audit reads the candidate, selected gaps, relevant sub-pattern cards, the Phase 0 evidence pool, the new collision hits, and the anti-pattern inventory. It runs four checks: gap-closure reject lessons, recipe application, anti-pattern substantive verification, and paper-pointed threat. It returns one of three verdicts, advance, revise, or abandon, plus concrete revision targets. The audit only judges; it does not edit the candidate. Third, and only when revision is possible, separate step applies the named edits or swaps in same-parent sub-pattern. It does not re-judge the verdict, change the selected gaps, or touch the kill-switch commitments. Splitting judging from fixing keeps attack-finding and attack-resolution separate, and keeps every failure mode tied to corpus evidence. Phase 3 audit details. The audit is single judgment step. It performs four corpus-anchored checks, applies two-layer verdict, and emits revision targets when bounded repair is possible. Each check requires concrete evidence from the retrieved literature, sub-pattern cards, or anti-pattern inventory. This makes the audit inspectable and resistant to rubber-stamping. The audit judges whether the candidate survives known failure modes. It is not guarantee of novelty, acceptance, or endpoint idea quality. Check 1: gap-closure-scoped reject scan. What it catches: candidates that look novel from the outside but fall into corpus-documented reject patterns specific to the chosen tactic. For each proposed gap closure, the audit reads the relevant sub-patterns failure-mode panel and rejected-paper lessons. It evaluates whether the candidate clearly avoids the lesson, clearly matches it, or sits in borderline state, and it must cite the candidate component that triggers the concern. Check 2: recipe application. What it catches: candidates that cite real sub-pattern but only execute its parent patterns generic idea rather than the clusters distinctive move, the leading cause of incremental output. Against the cited sub-pattern cards signature-move panel, the audit asks whether the core mechanism actually performs that move or merely gestures at the parent; an absent move marks the candidate bypassed. This is the semantic backstop the deterministic citation gate (13.3) cannot provide: the gate proves only that the citation resolves to real cluster under the right parent, never that the mechanism enacts the clusters tactic. Check 3: anti-pattern substantive verification. What it catches: keyword-stuffed mitigations, candidates whose core mechanism contains the right mitigation phrases without producing the artifact those phrases name. If the candidate uses one of the reject-enriched two-pattern compositions documented in Section 6.3, the audit 33 verifies that the required mitigation is substantively delivered in the mechanism, rather than through wording alone in the candidate. Check 4: paper-pointed threat. What it catches: candidates whose claim is already subsumed by specific recent paper, whether retrieved by Phase 0s broad-domain queries or by Phase 3s mechanism-specific collision retrieval. If paper that subsumes or competes with the candidates claim is found, the audit emits the threat paper, one-paragraph subsumption argument citing the threats specific result, and note on what would need to change. If no paper-pointed threat exists after honest search, the absence is legitimate clearance signal; fabricating generic threat to fill the audit is forbidden. Why saturation is not gated here. An earlier design added fifth saturation-defense check. We removed it because it consulted no retrieved related work. It was an advisory prior, not corpus-anchored fact, and therefore did not belong beside the four hard-floor checks above. Pattern saturation is still computed in Phase 1 and recorded in Phase 2. It now surfaces downstream as Phase 4 reviewer concern rather than gating the audit. This keeps every Phase 3 check anchored on retrieved evidence. Two-layer verdict. The verdict has two layers. Layer 1 is hard floor the model cannot override. It abandons the candidate when reject lesson clearly matches, when reject-enriched composition has no insertable mitigation, or when recent paper has exact-mechanism overlap. Layer 2 is model judgment within the safe zone Layer 1 leaves. It chooses advance or revise based on whether borderline findings touch load-bearing structural properties. recipe-application bypass always routes to revise: either swap to the sibling sub-pattern whose move the mechanism actually performs, or rework the mechanism to perform the cited move. If neither fits under the same parent, the run returns to Phase 2 regeneration rather than patching in place. The verdict must cite specific check findings; all checks pass without naming one is process error. 13.3.5 Phase 4: Expansion, implementability audit, rendering, and validation Phase 4 turns the candidate into the final idea-card artifact bundle. It expands the canonical candidate, runs default-on implementability audit, renders side artifacts, and then runs deterministic validators. The expansion derives the motivation, method flow, feasibility, reviewer concerns, literature differentiation, and domain-landscape explanation from the candidate plus the Phase 3 audit report. The implementability audit is single bounded LLM call. For each method step, it states how to build that step and flags anything left underspecified. It is written to separate file. By construction, that file cannot carry the protected falsification and compute-budget commitments. The audit therefore adds buildability detail without changing the claim. If run skips it, the implementability validators are not enabled. Rendering is deterministic templating. The renderer builds the Markdown and LaTeX artifacts, and compiles PDF when local TeX engine is available. Because the templates are fixed, validators can compare protected commitments against the pre-render candidate and check that required sections are present before the user sees the card. Phase 4 validation. suite of deterministic validators runs around the Phase 4 render path to check contracts asserted by earlier phases. They are mechanical. They detect violations through exact-text comparison, section presence, list length, schema coverage, readability checks, and citation consistency, not model judgment. Kill-switch integrity (hard fail). The candidates falsification prediction and compute budget must be preserved from initial candidate generation through final rendering. The validation chain depends on the verdict path: direct expansion when the audit advances, or revised candidate followed by expansion when the audit requests bounded fix. The Phase 3 revision step is forbidden from modifying these two commitments. The validator catches any drift mechanically rather than relying on prompt compliance. Expansion completeness (hard fail). Phase 4 expansion must contain the structural sections the ideacard rendering depends on: motivation with multiple prior-work stopping points, method-flow steps, feasibility validation with component-level and overall verdicts, non-empty proposal summary, core claim, and sub-claims. The check is section presence and list length. Absence is hard fail because missing sections would silently render as blank content in the final artifacts. 34 contract checks. Three Additional contracts. subpattern citation consistency runs immediately after Phase 2.2 and before any Phase 3 work. It checks that each cited sub-pattern resolves to real cluster under the stated parent pattern. implementability completeness confirms that the audit covers every method step when Phase 4.1.5 ran. implementability readability flags known readability regressions in the standard-register buildability text. validators pipeline further harden the What the validators do not check. They do not enforce semantic correctness inside section, they do not check whether the candidates claim is actually novel, and they do not enforce cross-run determinism. The phase calls are LLM-reasoning steps with non-zero variance, so the same query can yield different but coherent candidates across runs; this is expected for an inference-time skill rather than defect, and reporting distributions over repeated runs or seed-ensembled selection would make the variance explicit (15).

sections.title: 13.4 Output surface
sections.content: The user-facing deliverable is deliberately slimmed to the three things reader first judges: title, motivation, and method. An earlier, richer card printed the hook, claims, rebuttals, and provenance together. That version buried the idea under its own audit log. The current card is emitted in English and Chinese. Heavier material remains in the artifact bundle: the hook, problem-to-outcome summary, explicit claims, feasibility verdicts, literature-differentiation deltas, reviewer concerns and responses, and the literature-breakdown note. This lets an inspector trace why the candidate survived without turning the card into an audit dump. condensed generated card held-out ICLR 2026 seed: RL for long-context reasoning Title. Phase-Stratified Credit: Verifier-Free Per-Phase Advantage for Long-Context Reasoning RL (method: PS-GRPO). Motivation (the bottleneck). GRPO collapses each long rollout into one group-normalized score and broadcasts it uniformly over every token. But only few phaseswhere the model traces the key chain and grounds factdecide correctness, while most tokens scan distractor text. As context grows toward 128K, the decisive phases stay near-constant budget while the filler grows, so the useful signal per decisive token shrinks like 1/length: the method collapses exactly in the long-context regime it was built for. Method (step 1 of 4). Build the strongest equal-compute GRPO baseline; record each rollouts groupRq)/σq, broadcast uniformly to all Tg tokens, and its accuracy out to 128Kthe normalized advantage Ag = (Rg reference curve. [Three further steps harvest verifier-free per-phase credit density from the in-group correct/incorrect ordering; full card in App. A.] Falsification (kill-switch). Load-bearing variable: C, the fraction of advantage magnitude on the decisive phases. Negative control: permute the phase labels so the same lands on random spansif 128K accuracy survives, the gain came from generic variance reduction, not from localizing credit to the steps that determine correctness. (Full prediction with positive control in App. B.) The falsification prediction is single paragraph naming the minimal experiment and the metrics that should move. It gives qualitative direction but no pre-committed numerical thresholds. When the candidate makes mechanism claim, the schema requires one load-bearing variable: the quantity whose behavior carries the claim. It also requires negative-control intervention whose predicted effect lands on the downstream task outcome, not on the variables own definitional transform. This blocks recurring self-confirmation failure: intervene on so that becomes zero. That tests definition, not mechanism. Tying the control to an independent downstream effect makes the claim falsifiable. The distinguisher is required for mechanism candidates and optional for pure does this work? candidates. The output excludes experiment matrices, ablation plans, baseline tables, expected figures, and calendar projections. These belong to the users experimental engineering, not to the idea the skill produces.

sections.title: 13.5 Faithfulness: resisting hallucination
sections.content: The standing objection to LLM ideation is fabrication: invented citations, papers, numbers, or method detail IdeaSpark distributes its defenses across the phases in 13.3. The enforced asserted with false confidence. property is simple: every load-bearing claim either traces to retrieved record or is explicitly marked as not doing so. Grounding over memory. Retrieval is not left to the models discretion. Phase 0 follows fixed orchestration path rather than an in-context tool the model could replace with recall. Phase 1 refuses to start until the 35 grounding bundle and full-text cache are present, so bottleneck is not written from abstracts or memory alone. The method-lineage tree extends the same discipline backward in time. Ancestors supplied from model memory enter only as non-citable, awareness-only nodes. They make no novelty claim. subtractive gap may name only an assumption the retrieved methods actually exhibit. Citation faithfulness. deterministic gate checks every sub-pattern citation when the candidate is written. It halts the run on hallucinated parent, mis-filed sub-pattern, or an invented name before any costly Phase 3 retrieval is spent. The pattern cards carry paper-agnostic lessons with no per-paper identifiers. This removes, rather than merely polices, the surface on which paper id could be hallucinated. cleared audit may legitimately report no threat found; fabricating generic threat to fill the slot is forbidden. Claim faithfulness. Two guards bind the candidates own assertions. Quantitative claims about predicted behavior, such as rate, exponent, or scaling constant, must be cross-checked against same-setting papers in the full-text cache. The claim must then either align to that value or state the regime difference. This closes the recurring failure of pasting number across incompatible regimes. The two kill-switch fields, falsification prediction and compute budget, are held byte-identical from generation through rendering by hard validator. The model cannot silently soften the claim that makes the idea testable. Honest abstention. When the evidence does not support proposal, the run stops with do not generate or phase 3 failed report rather than producing one anyway. When the implementability audit reaches loadbearing detail it cannot supply without guessing, it records that detail as an open author decision. An honest gap is treated as more valuable than fluent filler. Together, these mechanisms turn do not hallucinate from an instruction the model may ignore into property enforced by retrieval gates, deterministic validators, and provenance discipline.

sections.title: 14 Evaluation: Generated-Idea Quality and Novelty
sections.content: This section reports an automated endpoint evaluation. It compares IdeaSpark against baselines on two axes, quality and novelty, using two auxiliary judging skills rather than human reviewers. The headline result is twofold. First, at matched length and format, judged blind, IdeaSparks idea cards receive the highest quality scores by wide margin while remaining competitively novel. Second, novelty alone is misleading. The evaluation exposes novel-but-empty failure mode, in which an idea appears highly novel because it is too vague to collide with prior art. We therefore treat the qualitynovelty plane as the primary readout. As discussed in 15, both judges are LLM-based skills, so this is an automated-judge study, not human acceptance study.

sections.title: 14.1 Systems compared
sections.content: We compare four idea sources on shared set of problem directions  (Table 10)  . The comparison points form ladder of controls around IdeaSpark. IdeaSpark is the system of this report. Opus-self-gen is generic ideation skill automatically authored by an Opus skill-creator, with no corpus grounding. Comparing it with IdeaSpark isolates the effect of our corpus-grounded skill from the effect of merely having some structured skill. Opus-4.8 (bare) is single prompt with no skill and no retrieval. IdeaSpark, Opus-self-gen, and Opus-4.8 (bare) all run on the same Opus 4.8-high backbone. Together they form same-backbone ladder: bare model, generic auto-built skill, and corpus-grounded skill. GPT-5.5 (bare) is second bare baseline from different model family, included to separate backbone-specific effects from skill effects.

sections.title: 14.2 Problem seeds: 100 method-agnostic directions from ICLR 2026
sections.content: Each system is asked to produce one idea per problem seed. We construct 100 seeds from ICLR 2026 Oral acceptances retrieved through the OpenReview API. ICLR 2026 is chosen deliberately: it post-dates the induction corpus (20212025) and the assumed training data of the generators and judges. The seeds therefore act as forward, held-out test rather than rediscovery exercise. To avoid leaking each papers own solution into the prompt, each seed is method-agnostic rewrite generated from the title alone. The rewrite keeps the domain object and task or capability, but strips method suffixes and any abstract-derived bottleneck diagnosis. For example, paper title containing specific mechanism for physical realism in video becomes the seed novel ML research idea about physical realism in text-to-video 36 Table 10: The four idea sources compared. Skill = produced by structured multi-phase skill; Retrieval = the generator read live literature while producing the idea. Source IdeaSpark Opus-self-gen Opus-4.8 (bare) GPT-5.5 (bare) What it is Skill Retrieval corpus-grounded multiThis reports method; phase skill, on Opus 4.8 high Generic ideation skill auto-authored by an Opus skill-creator run on cross-paper gap Opus 4.8 high: live web search (no corpus grounding), idea Bare Opus 4.8 high: single prompt, no skill, no retrieval Bare GPT-5.5: single prompt, no skill, no retrieval (different model family) models. One seed is generated per paper and screened for leakage. The resulting seeds name what problem to work on without hinting how.

sections.title: 14.3 Output normalization (controlling confounds)
sections.content: Quality and novelty judgments are sensitive to surface confounds, so all four sources are normalized to the same deliverable contract before judging. Every idea is converted to three-section Markdown document: Title, Motivation, and Method. Motivation is held to [259, 330] words and Method to [449, 866] words, matching the length distribution of the source ICLR Oral papers. This removes the length and verbosity confound that otherwise rewards longer write-ups. Every Method must also include formal equations with per-equation interpretation. This requirement matches the style of the source papers and puts vague prose and precise formalization on the same footing. The goal is to make judges score substance rather than surface technicality. We also strip protected author-decision annotations from IdeaSpark cards before scoring. These annotations are honest flags of unresolved design choices, but no other source emits them. Leaving them in would impose an asymmetric honesty tax; controlled comparison in 14.7 quantifies that effect.

sections.title: 14.4 Two automated judges
sections.content: Quality: the idea-quality skill. Quality is scored by self-contained skill that judges an idea from first principles on three axes, each assessable without experiments. The axes are (A) problem position, whether the attacked gap is real, important, and non-obvious rather than soft target; (B) method quality, decomposed into depth, soundness, and feasibility, with depth dominating; and (C) problem-fit, whether the method actually closes the stated gap. The skill requires quoted phrase of evidence for every axis score. It judges substance rather than length or fluency, and applies an A/C gate so that strong method on trivial problem cannot rank highly. For comparison, it runs in listwise mode, ranking several ideas for the same seed against one another while blind to source identity. Relative judgments are less noisy than absolute scores. Novelty: the scoop-check skill. Novelty is scored by prior-art collision skill. For each idea, the skill performs live literature search, deep-reads the closest candidates, and matches the proposed contribution against each prior work on four axes: problem framing, core mechanism, key insight, and application domain. The number of matching axes maps to five-level scale, level = 5 (axes matching). Thus Level 5 = no overlap (most novel) and Level 1 = all four axes match (fully scooped). The verdict for an idea is the worst case, or minimum level, over its closest retrieved prior works. We take the worst case rather than the mean because one sufficiently close prior work can scoop an idea. Averaging would let unrelated retrieved papers dilute decisive collision and inflate novelty. Higher is more novel, so novelty and quality point in the same direction.

sections.title: 14.5 Protocol and metrics
sections.content: Both axes use blind, repeated protocol over all 100 seeds. For each seed, the four systems ideas are copied to neutral filenames under fresh random label permutation. The shuffle differs by seed and by round to cancel 37 position bias. Source identity is withheld from the judge. Quality is listwise rank: rank 1 scores 4, rank 2 scores 3, rank 3 scores 2, and rank 4 scores 1. Novelty is the Level {1, . . . , 5} defined above. Each seed is judged in three independent rounds with fresh judge instances. For quality, we report each systems mean over the 100 seeds after averaging the three rounds per seed. For novelty, we report the mean level over all 300 judgments. Quality and novelty are run as separate passes.

sections.title: 14.6 Results
sections.content: Tables 11 and 12 report the two axes. Figure 1 places the four systems in the qualitynovelty plane. Figure 16 shows the full distribution of novelty levels. Wins is the number of seeds the system ranked first on its 3-round average (out of 100). The skill generators concentrate at L3 (medium overlap: shared framing/domain, distinct mechanism); the bare GPT-5.5 baseline piles up at L4, where its vagueness evades collision.

sections.title: 14.7 Analysis
sections.content: IdeaSpark wins on quality. IdeaSpark attains the highest quality score (3.87/4) and ranks first on 88 of the 100 seeds by 3-round average. No other system exceeds 2.6. The generic auto-authored skill, Opus-self-gen, scores 2.57, essentially tied with the bare Opus-4.8 baseline at 2.56. structured skill alone therefore does not lift idea quality. The same-backbone ladder isolates the effect of IdeaSparks specific design. IdeaSpark, Opus-self-gen, and Opus4.8 (bare) share one backbone (14.1). Opus-self-gen also has live retrieval. The observed gain is therefore not 38 explained by model family or by retrieval alone. The lift comes from IdeaSparks corpus-grounded pattern cards together with its multi-phase, audit-anchored workflow. Single-axis novelty is misleading. On novelty alone, the worst system wins. The bare GPT-5.5 baseline scores highest on novelty (3.73, with 214/300 judgments at L4), but is last on quality (1.00, zero wins). The judges rationales explain the mismatch. This baseline emits near-identical topic-agnostic template, generic diagnostic-heads + contrastive-pairs + uncertainty-routing scaffold that occasionally drifts off topic. Because the idea is vague, it has no precise prior art to collide with, so it scores as novel. This is the novel-but-empty regime. It is invisible to either axis alone. Only the qualitynovelty plane (Figure 1) separates genuine novelty, high on both axes, from vacuous novelty, high on novelty but low on quality. The two skill-based systems instead sit in the high-quality region while remaining at medium overlap (L3). Their ideas share problem framing and domain with the closest prior work but contribute distinct mechanism, an honest and defensible novelty profile. Calibration sub-study (honesty annotations). On 5-seed pilot, leaving IdeaSparks self-flagged authordecision annotations in the card cost roughly one rank position, dropping it behind the bare Opus-4.8 baseline. Removing them restored its lead. These annotations are author-facing meta-notes rather than part of the idea, and no other source emits them. This motivates the normalization in 14.3: strip the annotations before scoring.

sections.title: 15 Limitations
sections.content: Each limitation below is bounded property of the current study paired with concrete next step. None changes the claims we make, which remain scoped to the idea stage. They mark where additional evidence would extend those claims. Coverage is bounded by three ML conferences, which biases the induced distribution. The corpus is built entirely from ICLR, ICML, and NeurIPS outcomes and their public reviews, and the evaluation seeds are all ICLR 2026 Orals. The induced domains, the ideation patterns, and the implicit sense of what counts as strong direction therefore track the distribution of mainstream ML-conference research rather than science in general. Fields and venues under-represented in these proceedings are correspondingly under-covered, so generated ideas can inherit this bias and need not transfer to other communities. We write the patterns to be domain-agnostic to limit topic leakage, and the novelty pass checks overlap with live literature, but we do not enforce as-of backdating of the retrieval window. Cross-venue and cross-discipline corpora, backdated retrieval for leakage-free forward-prediction variant, and cross-backbone judges are the direct ways to test and widen external validity. The endpoint evaluation is automated, with human study as the next stage. Both axes are scored by LLM-based skills, so the results measure agreement with structured automated reviewers rather than human program-committee judgment, and the judges may share blind spots with the generators. An automated study is the appropriate first stage at the idea level; blind expert study is the planned next step before any acceptance-level claim. The judge metrics carry two known biases. First, idea-quality rewards novel mechanism, so benchmark, systems, and pure-measurement contributions score lower on its method-depth axis by construction; we therefore read quality as relative among generation systems, not as an absolute measure of research value. Second, novelty is meaningful only jointly with quality, since sufficiently generic idea can evade prior-art collision and score as novel (the GPT-5.5 case), which is why we never rank on novelty alone but report it as one axis of qualitynovelty plane. Rubrics for resource and systems contributions, and specificity-aware novelty metric that penalizes under-committed ideas, would reduce both biases.

sections.title: 16.1 Data card
sections.content: The corpus (Section 3) uses public metadata and public review material. Author identities are retained as paper metadata but are not used as model features for pattern induction or recommendation.

sections.title: 16.2 Model and backend card
sections.content: The current pipeline uses LLMs for extraction, taxonomy induction, card generation, and several skill phases, plus an embedding model for clustering. The principal models are Claude Sonnet 4.6 for signature extraction and paper-level tagging; Claude Opus 4.7 for taxonomy / domain induction and pattern-card generation; OpenAI text-embedding-3-large for strategy-signature clustering; and Claude Opus 4.8 high for the skill runtime and endpoint evaluation in 14. GPT-5.5 is used only as the cross-family baseline. These choices are backend-specific, and cross-backend quality and format-validity measurement remains future work.

sections.title: 16.3 Skill card
sections.content: IdeaSpark is appropriate for literature-grounded ML research directions where the user can state domain, adjacent literature, constraints, and desired contribution type. It is not appropriate for confidential literature without connector permission, broad agenda-setting prompts with no adjacent papers, pure benchmark construction, complete paper writing, automated submission production, or claims requiring private peer-review data. It returns structured idea card, do not generate diagnosis, or phase 3 failed report, but it cannot guarantee novelty, acceptance, feasibility, or reviewer agreement. Users remain responsible for prior-art review, experimental design, feasibility assessment, ethical review, and downstream claims.

sections.title: 17 Responsible Use
sections.content: Research-ideation systems reduce the cost of producing plausible-looking proposals, which also increases misuse risks: low-quality proposal generation, reviewer burden, shallow arXiv submissions, and overconfident novelty claims. IdeaSpark is therefore scoped as an ideation scaffold, not paper-writing agent. It outputs one idea card rather than manuscript, includes failure paths, audits candidates against corpus-derived reject lessons and prior-art threats, and keeps falsification and feasibility checks visible. Because the method draws on rejected submissions and reviews, we treat that signal as sensitive. Rejected papers are used only as aggregate, pattern-level evidence about how strategic moves fail, never to rank, profile, or single out papers, authors, or reviewers. Author identities are retained only as metadata and are not model features (3.2 / 16). Public deployment should include rate limits, artifact provenance, citation grounding, warnings that the system does not predict acceptance, and release rules for review-text redistribution and removal requests.

sections.title: 18 Conclusion
sections.content: We presented ResearchStudio-Idea, three-skill suite for the first mile of literature-grounded ML research ideation. Paper-Search provides standalone multi-source literature grounding, Scoop-Check provides standalone prior-art collision checking, and IdeaSpark composes these functions with outcome-grounded pattern guidance to produce one auditable idea card. The empirical contribution is 1,947-paper analysis of ICLR, ICML, and NeurIPS outcomes from 20212025, inducing 15 ideation patterns, 31 sub-patterns, and 28 research domains. The central system artifact is IdeaSpark: an operational card library and skill specification that make those patterns executable at inference time, with the same contrastive cards guiding bottleneck-to-move selection and candidate audit. The main claim is deliberately bounded. ResearchStudio-Idea does not show that an LLM can generate Oral papers, predict acceptance, or replace experimental judgment. It shows that public conference outcomes contain reusable strategic operators, that rejected and accepted papers often share those operators, and that their success and failure conditions can be packaged into practical skills for search, generation, and review. The automatedjudge evaluation in Section 14 provides initial endpoint evidence: at matched length and format, IdeaSpark improves judged idea quality over no-skill and generic-skill baselines while remaining competitively novel. The remaining gap is validation beyond the idea stage, including human reviewer preference, implementation success, and transfer outside the studied ML conference setting. Its value is to make pre-experimental ideation more structured, evidence-grounded, and auditable while leaving final prior-art review, experimental design, and scientific claims with the researcher.
